theory_query: Build a theory of how language model agents can best be augmented with memory to perform well at text games, based on the following results.

theory_statement_law.theory_name: Hybrid Structured and Episodic Memory Enables Robust, Generalizable LLM Agent Performance in Text Games
theory_statement_law.theory_description: Language model agents for text-based games achieve superior robustness, sample efficiency, and generalization when augmented with hybrid memory systems that combine explicit structured (e.g., knowledge-graph or belief-graph) state representations and episodic (trajectory or reflection-based) memory. Structured memory provides compact, relational representations that support multi-hop reasoning, navigation, and action-space pruning, while episodic memory enables recall of detailed instructions, procedures, and successful/failed experiences. The synergistic integration and principled retrieval of both kinds of memories enables agents to overcome both partial observability and long-horizon credit assignment bottlenecks, outperforming agents with either memory alone, with unstructured memory (e.g., full text history), or with naive retrieval/summarization.
theory_statement_law.theory_statement: When both structured (e.g., knowledge-graph/semantic) and episodic (e.g., observation/trajectory/reflective) memory are available and purposefully integrated and retrieved into agent working memory, LLM-based agents achieve higher normalized/game performance, reduced error rates, and greater sample efficiency and generalization compared to agents using only one memory type or only unstructured full-history/retrieval.
theory_statement_law.domain_scope: Applies to LLM agents in text-based games with partial observability, combinatorial action spaces, and complex state transitions, especially in environments requiring multi-step planning, navigation, and long-term instruction following (e.g., TextWorld, NetHack, general text-based tasks)
special_cases: Games solvable via short-term context or trivial observation-to-action mapping may show little benefit.
If memory construction (e.g., triplet extraction) is highly unreliable, benefits are reduced/disrupted.

phenomenon_effect.paper_title: AriGraph: Learning Knowledge Graph World Models with Episodic Memory for LLM Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper empirically shows that an LLM agent (Ariadne) augmented with a hybrid memory (AriGraph: semantic knowledge graph + episodic vertices/edges) attains substantially higher normalized scores and succeeds on long-horizon text-game tasks where baselines (full history, summarization, RAG, Reflexion, Simulacra, and several RL agents) largely fail. Ablations (w/o episodic, w/o exploration) and comparisons across multiple tasks (Treasure Hunt, Cleaning, Cooking, NetHack) demonstrate task-dependent contributions of components.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory statement asserts a general empirical regularity: purposefully integrating structured semantic memory and episodic memory into working memory yields higher performance, lower errors, and improved sample efficiency/generalization for LLM agents in text games compared to single-type or unstructured-memory agents.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper already states and experimentally supports this exact phenomenon across multiple benchmarks and ablations; thus the phenomenon claimed by the theory is explicitly established in the paper.

phenomenon_effect.paper_title: Deciphering Digital Detectives: Understanding LLM Behaviors and Capabilities in Multi-Agent Mystery Games

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper empirically demonstrates that adding an unstructured retrieval memory (vector DB) plus Self-Refinement (SR) and Self-Verification (SV) modules increases factual and inferential question accuracy, raises similarity between chat history and full scripts, and improves civilian win-rate and murderer-identification accuracy in the Jubensha multi-agent setting.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: It introduces and documents the empirical regularity that retrieval-augmented agents with iterative refinement and verification produce more authentic, informative responses and better task outcomes than memoryless baselines in this domain.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The specific phenomenon — that an unstructured episodic retrieval buffer combined with SR and SV yields measurable performance gains in Jubensha — is an explicit experimental finding of this paper and presented as a primary result.

phenomenon_effect.paper_title: Knowledge-enhanced Agents for Interactive Text Games

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper empirically demonstrates that injecting two external knowledge types—structured object affordances (from ConceptNet/KBs) and short-term episodic memory of previous correct actions (MCA)—into text-game agents improves task performance on many ScienceWorld tasks across four agent classes (DRRN, KG-A2C, RoBERTa, Swift). The experiments report aggregate and per-task improvements and show that affordances tend to produce larger gains than MCA and that combining both sometimes yields the best results.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The paper operationalizes and measures the performance effect of structured (affordance) and episodic (MCA) memory injections, both separately and combined, across multiple architectures and ten diverse ScienceWorld tasks, quantifying where each knowledge type helps or hurts.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The empirical claim that structured affordances plus episodic correct-action memory produce measurable, often complementary, performance gains (and that affordances are generally more effective than MCA on these benchmarks) is explicitly established in this paper for the tested models and tasks.

phenomenon_effect.paper_title: Positive Experience Reflection for Agents in Interactive Text Environments

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: Prior work focused on self-reflection from failures and storing reflections in long-term memory (e.g., Reflexion). The paper experimentally demonstrates that adding positive-experience reflection plus a managed short-/long-term reflection buffer (Sweet&Sour) improves average success scores on the ScienceWorld benchmark across three LLM families.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: Sweet&Sour introduces the empirical effect that explicitly reflecting on successful sub-goals (in addition to failures) and using managed memory increases task performance, reduces failure ‘tilt’, and yields proportionally larger gains for smaller LLMs on ScienceWorld.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The specific empirical phenomenon — consistent across tasks and model sizes within ScienceWorld — that positive-experience reflections combined with a dual-buffer managed memory produce measurable performance and robustness improvements is established by this paper.

phenomenon_effect.paper_title: A Mixture-of-Experts Approach to Few-Shot Task Transfer in Open-Ended Text Worlds

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper empirically demonstrates that an attention-weighted Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) composed of multiple frozen, role-aligned KG-A2C experts plus a single trainable ('hot') expert obtains higher zero-shot performance, faster few-shot reward discovery (greater sample efficiency), and higher final scores on blended and partial-blend role tasks in a LIGHT-derived open-ended text environment than single-expert baselines or training from scratch.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: It introduces the empirical effect that combining frozen role experts with a learnable expert and value-seeded attention produces robust few-shot task transfer and specific robustness patterns (sensitivity to distractor/irrelevant experts, failure under adversarial removal).
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The phenomenon—the MoE synergy (frozen experts + hot expert + value seeding + attention) yielding improved few-shot transfer in KG-A2C text-role environments—is an explicit, central experimental finding of this paper and is established within the paper's domain and benchmarks.

phenomenon_effect.paper_title: Learning Dynamic Belief Graphs to Generalize on Text-Based Games

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: This paper provides empirical evidence that a learned, continuous multi-relational belief-graph (dynamic structured memory) derived from partial text observations improves normalized test performance and generalization across a large distribution of TextWorld games compared to strong text-only baselines. It also shows that access to exact ground-truth graphs yields an even higher performance upper bound. The effect is measured across hundreds of unique games and multiple difficulty levels.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The paper explicitly introduces and documents the phenomenon that learned graph-structured belief states act as an effective memory representation that materially improves policy learning and generalization in TextWorld. It names and operationalizes this effect via the GATA agent and ablations (OG/COC pretraining, discrete vs continuous updaters).
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The phenomenon (learned continuous belief graphs improving generalization in text-game families) is an explicit, central empirical contribution of the paper and is established by its experiments.

phenomenon_effect.paper_title: A Survey on Large Language Model-Based Game Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The survey documents that memory is a central component of LLM-based game agents, distinguishing working vs long-term memory and multiple storage formats (chunks, key–value, trees, graphs, parametric), and it reports agent-level examples where memory mechanisms (e.g., reflection, Last-Thoughts, external knowledge retrieval) improve decision consistency and task performance in particular environments.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The evaluated theory asserts a concrete empirical phenomenon: purposefully integrating structured (knowledge-graph/semantic) and episodic (trajectory/reflective) memories into working memory yields higher normalized/game performance, lower error rates, and greater sample efficiency and generalization than agents using only one memory type or only unstructured full-history retrieval.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper catalogs separate uses and benefits of structured and episodic memories and presents per-agent empirical cases, but it does not provide an explicit cross-study empirical claim or meta-analysis demonstrating that a hybrid structured+episodic integration systematically outperforms single-type or unstructured baselines across games; the specific general performance law is therefore not explicitly established in the survey.

phenomenon_effect.paper_title: MemInsight: Autonomous Memory Augmentation for LLM Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper demonstrates empirically that structured, attribute-based augmentations of LLM agent memory improve retrieval effectiveness and downstream task performance (QA, conversational recommendation, event summarization) relative to baselines using raw/unstructured history or standard RAG; improvements appear across multiple metrics (F1/recall and LLM-based subjective metrics).
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory statement claims a broader phenomenon: that a hybrid memory combining explicit structured representations and episodic (trajectory/experience) memory purposefully integrated into working memory yields higher task performance, robustness, sample efficiency, and generalization in LLM agents operating in text-based games and related partially observable, long-horizon tasks.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: Extending the paper's demonstrated benefit of structured augmentations in dialogue tasks to a general hybrid-memory phenomenon (structured + episodic) in interactive text-game environments is not established by the paper; the empirical results support structured-memory benefits but do not show the synergistic role of a distinct episodic subsystem in text games.

phenomenon_effect.paper_title: Learning Knowledge Graph-based World Models of Textual Environments

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper demonstrates empirically that using an explicit structured memory in the form of knowledge graphs (with a Worldformer that predicts graph differences) substantially improves knowledge-graph and valid-action prediction metrics over unstructured/extractive baselines on a suite of text games. It also shows that multi-task learning and an SOS loss further increase prediction accuracy and make the modeling problem more tractable. The work establishes that structured graph representations enable useful look-ahead (e.g., inferring chest contents) not available to simple extractive approaches.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory statement claims a distinct empirical phenomenon: LLM agents that combine structured (knowledge-graph) and episodic (trajectory/reflective) memory and purposefully integrate retrieval achieve higher gameplay-normalized performance, lower error rates, and greater sample efficiency/generalization than agents with only one memory type or only unstructured full-history retrieval.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The specific empirical effect — measurable performance and sample-efficiency gains from a hybrid structured+episodic memory architecture in LLM agents playing text games — is not reported or evaluated in the paper. The paper documents benefits of structured memory alone but does not test or quantify the proposed hybrid-memory phenomenon.

phenomenon_effect.paper_title: Generative Agents: Interactive Simulacra of Human Behavior

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper demonstrates empirically that an LLM-driven agent architecture that records natural-language observations (memory stream), synthesizes periodic higher-level reflections, and produces hierarchical plans yields substantially more believable, coherent, and socially emergent behavior than ablated variants lacking these components; this is shown via interview-based believability rankings (TrueSkill) and a 25-agent two-day sandbox deployment with measured information diffusion, relationship formation, and coordination.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The Hybrid Memory Synergy Law asserts a broader empirical regularity: agents that combine explicit structured (knowledge-graph/semantic) memory and episodic (observation/trajectory/reflection) memory with principled joint retrieval will achieve higher normalized/game performance, lower error rates, greater sample efficiency, and improved generalization than agents using only one memory type or only unstructured full-history retrieval.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper provides strong evidence for the benefit of episodic natural-language memory plus reflection and planning, but it does not evaluate or demonstrate the specific additional benefit claimed for integrating an explicit structured semantic/graph memory (e.g., multi-hop KG retrieval, action-space pruning) with episodic traces; that combined structured+episodic performance claim is not empirically shown here.

phenomenon_effect.paper_title: Monte Carlo Planning with Large Language Model for Text-Based Game Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper empirically shows that integrating an LLM prior with short in-trial trajectory context and cross-trial episodic reflections into MCTS (MC-DML) yields substantially higher initial planning performance and better handling of bottleneck states across nine Jericho text games compared to multiple RL, KG-based, and LLM/MCTS baselines; ablations demonstrate performance drops when either memory component is removed. Prior work has separately reported benefits of knowledge-graph state representations and of LLM-based reflection, but not this exact MC-DML configuration.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: It introduces the concrete empirical phenomenon that LLM-guided MCTS augmented with both an in-trial short trajectory window and cross-trial textual reflections produces large gains in initial (iteration-1) game performance and sample efficiency on the tested Jericho suite.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper documents a clear empirical effect for episodic (reflection) + short-term context memory in LLM+MCTS agents, but it does not provide empirical evidence for the specific hybrid phenomenon claimed by the theory that combines structured (KG-like) memory with episodic memory; that structured+episodic phenomenon is not demonstrated here.

phenomenon_effect.paper_title: RAP: Retrieval-Augmented Planning with Contextual Memory for Multimodal LLM Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper empirically demonstrates that storing and retrieving prior successful episodes (episodic trajectory logs) and presenting windowed trajectory examples as in‑context demonstrations improves agent success rates, reward scores, and sample efficiency across multiple textual and multimodal benchmarks (ALFWorld, WebShop, Franka Kitchen, Meta-World). It also shows that selective retrieval keys, adaptive similarity weighting, and windowing reduce noise and aid performance.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory statement claims a distinct phenomenon: a synergistic performance boost when an explicit structured (e.g., knowledge-graph/semantic) memory is combined with episodic (trajectory/experience) memory and jointly retrieved into working memory, outperforming episodic-only, structured-only, or unstructured full-history approaches.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper supports the empirical effect of episodic retrieval but does not implement, compare, or empirically validate an explicit structured relational memory (e.g., KG) nor the incremental benefit of a KG+episodic hybrid. Thus the specific empirical phenomenon (hybrid structured+episodic synergy) is not established in the paper.

phenomenon_effect.paper_title: Large Language Models Are Neurosymbolic Reasoners

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper empirically demonstrates that zero-shot prompted LLM agents (GPT-3.5/GPT-4) that can call external symbolic modules (calculator, navigator, sorter, knowledge-base) achieve high normalized game performance (average ≈0.88 on the tested benchmarks) and outperform or match RL and behavior-cloned baselines on several symbolic text-game tasks. It reports task-specific failure modes (e.g., forgetting navigation routes, limited internal memory for sorting) that affect agent efficiency and accuracy.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The Hybrid Memory Synergy Law asserts a distinct empirical effect: agents that purposefully integrate both structured (knowledge-graph/semantic) and episodic (trajectory/reflective) memories into working memory will reliably obtain higher normalized performance, lower error rates, and greater sample efficiency and generalization than agents with only one memory type or only unstructured full-history retrieval.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper does not present experiments or empirical measurements comparing combined structured+episodic memory augmentation to single-memory or unstructured-history baselines; it evaluates prompting plus external symbolic modules but not a hybrid structured/episodic memory architecture. Therefore the specific empirical phenomenon claimed by the Hybrid Memory Synergy Law is not observed or tested in this work.

phenomenon_effect.paper_title: Graph Constrained Reinforcement Learning for Natural Language Action Spaces

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper empirically demonstrates that a dynamically built structured memory (a knowledge graph) used as the agent state, combined with a template-based action space and graph-based masking, improves performance and exploration efficiency across a suite of 28 interactive-fiction games versus a strong template-based baseline (TDQN). Ablations show that graph-derived components accelerate learning and that removing template structure (full vocabulary decoding) degrades performance severely.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The submitted theory claims a distinct empirical phenomenon: that agents having both structured (knowledge-graph) and episodic (trajectory/reflective) memories purposefully integrated achieve synergistic gains in normalized/game performance, lower error rates, and greater sample efficiency and generalization compared to agents with only one memory type or only unstructured full-history retrieval.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper provides clear evidence for benefits of structured memory but contains no experiments or claims about episodic-memory stores or their integration with structured memory; therefore the specific hybrid-structured+episodic phenomenon asserted by the theory is not evidenced in this paper.

phenomenon_effect.paper_title: Behavior Cloned Transformers are Neurosymbolic Reasoners

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper demonstrates empirically that injecting actions from symbolic modules (arithmetic, navigation, sorting, knowledge-base lookup) into the action space yields large, consistent performance and efficiency gains for a behavior-cloned transformer across four text-game benchmarks, while the DRRN baseline does not benefit under the reported training regime.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The evaluated theory claims a named phenomenon: that purposeful joint availability and retrieval of structured (knowledge-graph/semantic) and episodic (trajectory/reflective) memory produces superior normalized/game performance, lower error rates, and improved sample efficiency and generalization relative to single-memory or unstructured-history agents.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper shows benefits of structured symbolic tool injection plus supervised trajectories but does not test or name episodic-memory retrieval/integration at inference; therefore the specific hybrid-memory phenomenon asserted by the theory (structured+episodic synergy at retrieval time) is not demonstrated in this work.

phenomenon_effect.paper_title: Reasoning, Memorization, and Fine-Tuning Language Models for Non-Cooperative Games

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper empirically demonstrates that a multi-agent, tree-of-thought decomposition (summarization, area selection, action selection, validation) yields higher win rates (~60–65%), lower error rates, and sample-efficient fine-tuning (~1k examples yields ≈10% improvement) on the introduced Goedendag game compared to direct single-model and CoT baselines. Ablations show removing representation/area-selection tasks degrades win rate and increases error rate. The paper thus establishes an empirical effect for this particular pipeline and game domain.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory statement claims a distinct phenomenon: that agents explicitly combining structured (knowledge-graph/semantic) memory and episodic (trajectory/experience) memory, with principled retrieval into working memory, outperform agents with only one memory type or with naive unstructured history retrieval.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper does not implement, compare, or measure separate structured versus episodic memory stores or their joint effect; it uses compact text-based representations and task decomposition but provides no experiments isolating an episodic memory component or hybrid-structured+episodic architectures. Therefore the specific phenomenon claimed by the theory (hybrid structured+episodic memory superiority) is not present in the paper and is novel relative to it.

phenomenon_effect.paper_title: Reflexion: Language Agents with Verbal Reinforcement Learning

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper empirically demonstrates that storing natural-language self-reflections (episodic textual memory) and conditioning subsequent trials on them yields large, consistent improvements across decision-making (AlfWorld), multi-hop reasoning (HotPotQA), and code-generation (HumanEval/MBPP/Leetcode) benchmarks, with quantitative gains and ablations isolating the self-reflection step.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The evaluated theory claims a distinct phenomenon: a synergistic performance boost when explicit structured (e.g., knowledge-graph/semantic) memory is combined with episodic (trajectory/reflective) memory and both are purposefully integrated and retrieved into working memory.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: Reflexion documents episodic/verbal-memory benefits but does not introduce, measure, or report any phenomenon involving combined structured+episodic memory; the hybrid-effect claim is absent from the paper's experiments and therefore novel relative to this paper.

phenomenon_effect.paper_title: Language Models are Few-Shot Butlers

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: This paper empirically demonstrates that pretrained autoregressive LMs fine-tuned on a small set of expert demonstrations and improved via iterated action modeling (self-generated successful trajectories) achieve large absolute gains in success rate and strong sample efficiency on the ALFWorld text environment, including some out-of-distribution human-annotated goals.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory statement asserts a specific empirical phenomenon: that combining structured (knowledge-graph/semantic) memory and episodic (trajectory/reflective) memory, with purposeful integration and retrieval into working memory, produces higher normalized/game performance, lower error rates, and better sample efficiency/generalization than using only one memory type or only unstructured full-history retrieval.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper provides evidence for gains from pretrained LMs and episodic-like replay (IAM) but does not construct, evaluate, or report results for explicit structured-memory representations or their hybrid integration with episodic memory; hence the hybrid structured+episodic phenomenon claimed by the theory is not observed in this work.

phenomenon_effect.paper_title: Thespian: Multi-Character Text Role-Playing Game Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper empirically demonstrates that a single thespian agent using soft character prompts and per-character action logits can match or exceed single-character baselines on character-specific objectives in the LIGHT environment, and that a frozen core plus a small attention fusion module enables fast few-shot learning of a blended character while preserving prior skills.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The submitted theory asserts a broader empirical phenomenon: that hybrid memory systems combining explicit structured (knowledge-graph) and episodic (trajectory/reflection) memory, with principled retrieval into working memory, yield superior robustness, sample efficiency, and generalization versus single-memory or unstructured full-history approaches in text-game LLM agents.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper reports improved multi-character policy fusion via prompt-conditioning and attention, but it does not present experiments or measurements that demonstrate the specific phenomenon of combined structured+episodic memory providing the claimed synergy; the hybrid-memory empirical claim is not established here.

phenomenon_effect.paper_title: diff History for Neural Language Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper provides strong empirical evidence that replacing full-text observation histories with Unix-style text deltas ('diff history') yields large improvements in sample efficiency, context-token compression (in high-dimensional settings), and downstream agent performance in procedurally-generated text environments (notably matching neural-agent SOTA on NetHack with far fewer labels). It reports ablations showing severe performance drops when diffs are removed and quantifies effects across BabyAI-Text and NetHack.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory statement claims a different empirical phenomenon: that purposefully integrating both explicit structured (knowledge/graph) memory and episodic (trajectory/reflective) memory into LLM agent working memory produces superior normalized/game performance, lower error rates, greater sample efficiency, and better generalization than using either memory type alone or unstructured full-history retrieval.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper documents that a particular form of structured episodic encoding (text diffs) improves agent performance, but it does not measure or compare explicit graph-like structured memory, nor tests combined structured+episodic architectures. Therefore the specific phenomenon that a hybrid structured+episodic memory system outperforms single-memory or naive full-history agents is not demonstrated in the paper and remains a new empirical claim relative to its content.

phenomenon_effect.paper_title: Keep CALM and Explore: Language Models for Action Generation in Text-based Games

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: This paper empirically demonstrates that a pretrained language model (CALM) trained on diverse human gameplay, when used to generate top-k action candidates and combined with a DRRN re-ranker, yields substantially better average normalized game score (≈69% relative improvement over the NAIL baseline) and can in many cases match or outperform agents that use ground-truth admissible-action handicaps. It also shows empirical regularities about quality–quantity tradeoffs (top-k affects precision/recall of admissible and gold actions) and that pretraining plus ClubFloyd fine-tuning are important. The paper documents that LM priors can produce many admissible and contextually-relevant actions across unseen games.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The Hybrid Memory Synergy Law asserts a new empirical phenomenon: purposefully integrating both structured (knowledge-graph) and episodic (trajectory/reflective) memories into agent working memory yields higher normalized/game performance, lower error rates, and better sample efficiency and generalization than any single memory type or naive full-history retrieval.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The specific empirical effect that a principled, integrated hybrid of structured and episodic memories produces strictly superior and robust LLM-agent performance in text games is not claimed or demonstrated in this paper; the paper shows benefits of an unstructured LM+RL pipeline and reports results for a knowledge-graph based baseline, but never tests or documents the proposed hybrid-memory synergy as a phenomenon.

phenomenon_effect.paper_title: Pre-trained Language Models as Prior Knowledge for Playing Text-based Games

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper empirically demonstrates that augmenting DRRN/TDQN agents with a pre-trained transformer (DistilBERT) fine-tuned on human gameplay transcripts yields higher game scores, improved sample efficiency (fewer environment interactions than baselines), and modest cross-game transfer in Jericho text games; it reports SOTA on 4/14 games and higher average normalized score than several baselines. The observed effect is that a single LM-based representation improves semantic understanding and enables better action selection in partially observable text-game environments.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory claims a distinct empirical phenomenon: purposefully integrating both structured (knowledge-graph/semantic) memory and episodic (trajectory/reflective) memory into agent working memory produces systematically higher normalized/game performance, lower error rates, and better sample efficiency and generalization than using either memory type alone or only unstructured full-history retrieval.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper does not empirically test or report experiments that combine an explicit structured memory and an episodic memory with principled joint retrieval into working memory; therefore the specific hybrid structured+episodic performance effect asserted by the theory is not present in the paper and is a novel empirical claim relative to it.

explanatory_mechanistic.paper_title: AriGraph: Learning Knowledge Graph World Models with Episodic Memory for LLM Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper provides a concrete mechanistic account: semantic triplet extraction builds a KG (V_s,E_s), episodic vertices store raw observations (V_e) and episodic edges link triplets from the same timestep (E_e); retrieval is a two-stage semantic-then-episodic search with defined scoring heuristics, and planning/decision modules consume retrieved contents in working memory.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory attributes performance gains to complementary mechanisms: compact relational representations support multi-hop reasoning, navigation and action pruning, while episodic memory preserves detailed procedural/contextual traces that aid long-horizon credit and instruction following, and their principled joint retrieval enables synergy.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: Those causal/mechanistic elements (graph structure, episodic links, retrieval algorithms, and their roles in planning/decision making) are explicitly proposed, implemented, and used to explain empirical gains in the paper; the theory's mechanistic claims are therefore present and instantiated.

explanatory_mechanistic.paper_title: Knowledge-enhanced Agents for Interactive Text Games

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper attributes improvements to mechanisms such as action-space pruning via affordances, richer relational state representations via KGs, and reinforcement of successful decisions via MCA; these mechanistic rationales are described and connected to implementation choices (KG triples, separate encoders, GRU/MCA windows).
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: These mechanistic accounts are instantiated in the architectures (e.g., affordances added to KG vs. concatenated inputs; MCA encoded with GRU or appended text) and used to interpret per-model and per-task outcomes.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The explanations are plausible and align with existing intuitions about KGs and episodic memory; the paper does not propose a fundamentally new causal mechanism beyond applying known mechanisms to these injection designs and empirically verifying their impact.

explanatory_mechanistic.paper_title: A Survey on Large Language Model-Based Game Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The survey explicates mechanistic roles: structured memories (graphs, triplets, keys) support relational/multi-hop retrieval and compact state representations, while episodic memories (trajectories, reflections, summaries) capture ordered procedural experience, success/failure exemplars, and support reflective improvement; it also details consolidation, structuring, and retrieval mechanisms that mediate memory utility.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory offers a causal account claiming synergy: structured memory provides compact relational scaffolding for multi-step reasoning and action pruning, episodic memory provides detailed procedural recall and credit-assignment signals, and principled joint retrieval into working memory causally overcomes partial observability and long-horizon credit-assignment bottlenecks.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The survey already describes the separate functional contributions and retrieval/consolidation mechanisms, but it does not explicitly formulate or empirically validate the specific causal synergy claim (that the combined mechanism reliably resolves both partial observability and long-horizon credit assignment across tasks); thus the integrated causal claim is an inferred synthesis rather than an explicit established mechanism in the paper.

explanatory_mechanistic.paper_title: A Mixture-of-Experts Approach to Few-Shot Task Transfer in Open-Ended Text Worlds

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper provides mechanistic components for its results within the MoE: value seeding (multiplying expert action distributions by critic-predicted returns) biases attention toward experts with high expected return, an attention network projects observations and expert logits to select experts, and epsilon-greedy plus an entropy-like loss mitigate premature convergence and expert starvation; KG-A2C provides a persistent, structured KG state representation for each expert.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The submitted theory statement attributes the performance gains to a causal synergy between two distinct memory systems—structured relational memory and episodic/trajectory memory—when purposefully integrated into working memory; the paper does not implement or test an episodic-memory substrate or retrieval mechanism and thus does not present or validate this specific causal account.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The hybrid structured+episodic causal mechanism claimed by the theory is not proposed, instrumented, or experimentally evaluated in the paper; the paper's mechanistic explanations concern attention/value-seeding in an MoE and KG-based persistent memory only.

explanatory_mechanistic.paper_title: Reasoning, Memorization, and Fine-Tuning Language Models for Non-Cooperative Games

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper offers mechanistic explanations for its empirical gains: task decomposition reduces per-agent memory and cognitive load, summarization produces compact, consistent text-based state representations, area selection constrains the exponential state/action space (linear memory growth), and action validation reduces rule violations; fine-tuning via ranked self-play improves task-specific behavior.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory provides a different mechanistic account: structured memory enables multi-hop relational reasoning, navigation, and action-space pruning while episodic memory stores detailed trajectories, successes/failures and supports credit-assignment and recall; crucially, the retrieval policy integrating both into working memory resolves partial observability and long-horizon credit assignment.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper's mechanisms overlap with the structured-memory side (compact representations, pruning the search), but it does not define or test an episodic-memory mechanism nor the specific causal interaction (synergy) between structured and episodic stores. The hybrid causal account therefore is not articulated or experimentally validated in the paper and would need to be inferred or developed from multiple sources.

explanatory_mechanistic.paper_title: Monte Carlo Planning with Large Language Model for Text-Based Game Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper provides a mechanistic account for the observed improvement: failed simulated trajectories are summarized by the LLM into short textual reflections stored as cross-trial memory, and these reflections, together with an in-trial trajectory window, modulate the LLM prior over actions during subsequent MCTS simulations, thereby biasing exploration away from failure-prone high-immediate-reward actions and improving value estimates.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: It operationalizes a retrieval-and-reprompt mechanism where LLM-generated reflections are appended to memory and explicitly used in prompts to produce adjusted action probability priors inside PUCT-style selection.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: This paper supplies a concrete, tested mechanistic pathway for episodic/reflection-driven adjustment of LLM priors in planning, but it does not introduce or evaluate the mechanistic role of structured relational (knowledge-graph) memory nor the interaction mechanics between structured representations and episodic reflections described in the theory.

explanatory_mechanistic.paper_title: Learning Dynamic Belief Graphs to Generalize on Text-Based Games

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper provides a mechanistic account for why graph beliefs help: belief graphs encode entities and relations (supporting relational/multi-hop reasoning), the recurrent graph-updater accumulates temporal information to mitigate partial observability, and the graph-encoder + bi-attention aggregator guide action selection; self-supervised pretraining (OG and COC) is used to shape graph semantics. These components and their roles are described and ablated.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The submitted theory claims an additional mechanism: a distinct episodic memory (trajectory/experience/reflective store) integrated with structured KG-like memory and principled retrieval yields synergistic benefits (improved long-horizon recall, credit assignment, and robustness).
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper does not implement or evaluate a separate episodic-memory module nor the explicit structured–episodic synergy described in the theory; the hybrid mechanistic claim therefore extends beyond the paper’s implemented mechanisms and results.

explanatory_mechanistic.paper_title: Large Language Models Are Neurosymbolic Reasoners

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper offers mechanistic commentary at a high level: LLM agents leverage pretraining-derived patterns and invoke external symbolic modules as tools via prompting, and observed failures are partially explained by limited in-context/memory capacity (e.g., forgetting routes) and prompt design choices. The manuscript does not formalize internal memory architectures or retrieval mechanisms beyond prompting and tool invocation.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory proposes a mechanistic decomposition in which structured memory provides compact relational representations (supporting multi-hop reasoning, navigation, and action pruning) while episodic memory provides detailed procedural and outcome traces, and their principled retrieval into working memory resolves partial observability and long-horizon credit assignment.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: That specific causal account (how structured and episodic memories interact, what retrieval policies are required, and how this resolves partial observability/credit-assignment) is not articulated, modeled, or empirically tested in the paper; the paper instead focuses on tool-use via prompts without implementing or analyzing hybrid memory mechanisms.

explanatory_mechanistic.paper_title: Graph Constrained Reinforcement Learning for Natural Language Action Spaces

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper provides mechanistic explanations for how the knowledge graph helps: it yields a compact relational state representation, is embedded via GATs for multi-hop reasoning, and its graph mask prunes the combinatorial template action space to focus generation on actionable objects, which together improve exploration and policy learning.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory proposes a mechanistic account in which structured memory supplies compact relational representations for multi-hop reasoning and action-space pruning while episodic memory supplies detailed procedural/trajectory recall; importantly it asserts that principled retrieval and integration of both stores resolves partial observability and long-horizon credit assignment bottlenecks.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: While the paper explains mechanisms for structured memory alone and how it constrains generation, it does not propose, implement, or analyze episodic-memory mechanisms nor their retrieval/integration policies; the combined mechanistic account (structured↔episodic interplay and retrieval into working memory) is absent from the paper.

explanatory_mechanistic.paper_title: Deciphering Digital Detectives: Understanding LLM Behaviors and Capabilities in Multi-Agent Mystery Games

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper provides mechanistic descriptions for its modules: MR mitigates context-window limits by storing/retrieving past observations via embeddings, SR decomposes questions to retrieve missing script facts and augment answers, and SV breaks answers into facts and checks them against scripts to reduce hallucination.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The submitted theory asserts a distinct causal mechanism in which the combination of explicit structured (graph/relational) memory and episodic (trajectory/observation) memory, with principled retrieval and integration, jointly produces synergistic gains in multi-hop reasoning, navigation/action pruning, and long-horizon credit assignment.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper does not implement or analyze explicit structured (knowledge-graph/semantic) memory nor provide causal evidence for structured+episodic synergy; therefore the specific mechanistic claim about hybrid structured+episodic memory producing the listed causal benefits is not supported by this work.

explanatory_mechanistic.paper_title: MemInsight: Autonomous Memory Augmentation for LLM Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: MemInsight describes a concrete pipeline (attribute mining by perspective/granularity, priority-based annotation, attribute-filtering and embedding-based retrieval) and attributes improved performance to more precise, prioritized memory representations and retrieval.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory asserts a specific mechanistic decomposition: structured memory provides compact relational representations for multi-hop reasoning, navigation, and action-space pruning, while episodic memory supplies detailed trajectories, procedural recall, and failure/success traces; principled retrieval of both resolves partial observability and long-horizon credit assignment.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: Those specific causal roles and their interaction (structured→multi-hop/pruning, episodic→credit-assignment/procedural recall) are not articulated, measured, or ablated in the paper, so the proposed mechanism goes beyond the paper's implementation and experiments.

explanatory_mechanistic.paper_title: Positive Experience Reflection for Agents in Interactive Text Environments

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper provides a mechanistic explanation for Sweet&Sour: positive reflections reinforce successful strategies, the dual short-/long-term buffers preserve and surface useful reflections across attempts, and the richer context prevents early-success decline ('tilt').
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory statement claims a different mechanism: a synergistic causal interaction between structured relational memory (knowledge/belief graphs) that enables multi-hop reasoning and pruning, and episodic memory that supplies detailed trajectories and procedures; their principled joint retrieval into working memory resolves partial observability and long-horizon credit assignment.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper contains an episodic/managed-memory mechanistic account but contains no structured-graph memory component nor any analysis of structured+episodic synergy or how their joint retrieval causally improves planning. That structured+episodic mechanistic claim is absent from the paper.

explanatory_mechanistic.paper_title: Behavior Cloned Transformers are Neurosymbolic Reasoners

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper offers mechanistic explanations for its results: behavior cloning succeeds because gold trajectories teach the transformer to use module actions and pretrained knowledge aids some tasks, while the DRRN fails due to myopic reward signals and exploration difficulty in large action spaces; it also specifies the operational mechanism of action-injection (module actions become part of A_t and are executed by the module if selected).
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory proposes a distinct mechanistic decomposition: structured memory affords compact relational, multi-hop reasoning and action pruning, while episodic memory supplies detailed procedural recall and successful/failed experience traces, and their principled retrieval together resolves partial observability and long-horizon credit assignment.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: That particular causal account—how structured and episodic memories interact and why their integration specifically yields robustness, sample efficiency, and generalization—is not derived, tested, or articulated in the paper and therefore represents a new explanatory proposal relative to this work.

explanatory_mechanistic.paper_title: Reflexion: Language Agents with Verbal Reinforcement Learning

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: Reflexion supplies an explicit mechanistic account for why episodic verbal memory helps: sparse scalar/environment feedback is converted into actionable natural-language summaries (self-reflections) that are persisted and retrieved to improve credit assignment, error localization, and next-trial planning without weight updates.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory posits a new causal account in which structured representations provide compact relational state, multi-hop reasoning, and action-space pruning while episodic reflections supply detailed procedural recall, and principled retrieval/integration of both alleviates partial observability and long-horizon credit-assignment.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: While Reflexion explains the mechanism of episodic verbal reinforcement, it does not propose or test the specific causal interactions or integration protocols between structured relational state representations and episodic reflections; that mechanistic integration is not present in the paper and therefore constitutes a novel mechanistic claim.

explanatory_mechanistic.paper_title: Learning Knowledge Graph-based World Models of Textual Environments

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper provides mechanistic explanations for its results: knowledge graphs provide compact relational state representations that mitigate partial observability and combinatorial action spaces, predicting graph differences yields look-ahead, multi-task learning shares information between mapping and acting, and the SOS loss enforces permutation invariance of sets of sequences to improve decoder training.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory attributes causal roles to two distinct memory modalities: structured memory for multi-hop relational reasoning and action-space pruning, and episodic memory for recall of procedures, trajectories and failed/successful experiences; it further claims their principled integration and retrieval into working memory resolves long-horizon credit assignment and partial observability bottlenecks in LLM agents.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: That joint, causally complementary mechanisms of structured (KG) plus episodic memory produce the claimed improvements (specifically resolving long-horizon credit assignment and partial observability when integrated into LLM working memory) is not proposed or analyzed in the paper; this explanatory mechanism goes beyond the paper's mechanistic claims about structured memory and multi-task learning.

explanatory_mechanistic.paper_title: Language Models are Few-Shot Butlers

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper offers a mechanistic interpretation for its results: pretrained linguistic priors give action generation competence and iterated action modeling augments training data with successful trajectories (treating them as additional demonstrations), effectively combining imitation learning and an RL-style data collection loop under the same action-modeling objective to improve performance and sample efficiency.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory proposes a different causal mechanism: structured memory provides compact relational representations enabling multi-hop reasoning and action-space pruning, episodic memory supplies detailed traces of past interactions for recall and credit assignment, and principled retrieval that integrates both into working memory yields robustness and generalization.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: Those specific causal roles for structured representations, their interaction with episodic traces, and the claim that principled cross-memory retrieval is the mechanism of improved long-horizon performance are not proposed, analyzed, or tested in the paper, so the mechanistic account in the theory is novel relative to this work.

explanatory_mechanistic.paper_title: Generative Agents: Interactive Simulacra of Human Behavior

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper specifies mechanistic components for long-term coherence: a timestamped natural-language memory stream of observations, an LM-based retrieval scoring function combining recency/importance/relevance (embeddings + LM scoring), periodic reflection synthesis into higher-level abstract memories, and recursive top-down hierarchical planning with just-in-time decomposition and reactive updating; these mechanisms are used to explain why the full architecture outperforms ablations.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The Hybrid Memory Synergy Law posits mechanistically distinct roles for two memory modalities: structured semantic/state memory (supporting multi-hop reasoning, navigation grounding, and action-space pruning) and episodic trajectory/reflective memory (supporting detailed recall of events, procedures, and outcomes), and it claims principled joint retrieval of both modalities into working memory resolves partial observability and long-horizon credit assignment bottlenecks.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: While the paper details mechanisms for episodic natural-language memory, retrieval, reflection, and planning, it does not develop, implement, or evaluate the KG-like structured-memory mechanisms (semantic multi-hop graphs, graph-based pruning or retrieval) nor the claim that their integration resolves long-horizon credit assignment in combinatorial text-game settings; those mechanistic assertions are absent from the paper.

explanatory_mechanistic.paper_title: Thespian: Multi-Character Text Role-Playing Game Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper provides a concrete mechanism for the effects it measures: learnable soft character prompts, stacked per-character action logits and value outputs, and a frozen-core attention module that linearly combines raw logits to produce a blended policy for a new character, which explains fast adaptation and avoidance of catastrophic forgetting empirically.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory proposes a different mechanistic account in which structured memory enables compact relational, multi-hop reasoning and action pruning, episodic memory supplies procedural/outcome recall, and principled retrieval of both into working memory resolves partial observability and long-horizon credit assignment.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: That specific dual-memory causal mechanism (how KG-like structured state and separate episodic trajectory stores interact via retrieval to produce the claimed benefits) is not implemented, analyzed, or argued in the paper, so the mechanistic explanation is novel relative to this work.

explanatory_mechanistic.paper_title: diff History for Neural Language Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper offers mechanistic conjectures for why diff history helps: (1) task-agnostic relational abstraction that localizes salient changes and provides a denser learning signal for finetuning, and (2) token-level 'soft compression' that extends effective context length under fixed LM context budgets, both supported by data (token statistics, ablations).
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory proposes a distinct mechanistic account in which structured memory supplies compact relational, multi-hop reasoning and action-pruning capabilities while episodic memory supplies detailed procedural/experience recall; principled retrieval and integration of both resolve partial observability and long-horizon credit assignment via complementary functions.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: While the paper explains why a compressed, relational episodic representation (diffs) helps, it does not formalize or empirically test the complementary causal roles, retrieval policies, or integration mechanisms between an explicit structured (graph-like) memory and an episodic trace store. The proposed causal synergy and retrieval-integration dynamics are therefore mechanistically novel beyond the paper's analyses.

explanatory_mechanistic.paper_title: Keep CALM and Explore: Language Models for Action Generation in Text-based Games

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper offers a mechanistic explanation for CALM's effectiveness: pretrained LMs capture linguistic priors and human ‘game sense’ which substantially reduce the effective action space, and a DRRN trained on those candidates can learn to select high-value actions via RL; pretraining and diverse human transcripts provide useful inductive biases. It also analyzes operational mechanisms such as the top-k tradeoff and the choice not to fine-tune CALM during RL to avoid overfitting.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The Hybrid Memory Synergy Law proposes a different mechanistic account: structured memory gives compact relational representations enabling multi-hop reasoning and action pruning, episodic memory provides detailed procedural recall, and principled retrieval/integration of both resolves partial observability and long-horizon credit assignment.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: That particular causal/mechanistic decomposition (structured memory for relational multi-hop reasoning + episodic memory for procedural recall + a retrieval/integration protocol producing synergistic benefits) is not developed, tested, or explained in the paper; CALM's mechanism is LM-based candidate generation plus RL re-ranking, not a dual-memory retrieval/integration mechanism.

explanatory_mechanistic.paper_title: RAP: Retrieval-Augmented Planning with Contextual Memory for Multimodal LLM Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper provides a concrete mechanistic pipeline: store logs as structured records {task, overall plan, trajectory}, generate retrieval keys from a Reasoner, compute weighted similarity across task/plan/key (using text/image embeddings), retrieve a trajectory window, and use retrieved examples as in‑context prompts to guide action generation by the Executor.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory gives a mechanistic account attributing complementary causal roles to two memory types: structured memory supplies compact relational, multi-hop reasoning and action-space pruning, while episodic memory supplies detailed procedural recall; their principled joint retrieval purportedly resolves partial observability and long-horizon credit assignment.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper's mechanistic account covers episodic retrieval → in-context guidance but does not implement or validate the claimed causal roles of an explicit structured relational memory (multi-hop pruning, relational reasoning) nor demonstrate that combining the two memories resolves long‑horizon credit assignment in the way the theory asserts.

explanatory_mechanistic.paper_title: Pre-trained Language Models as Prior Knowledge for Playing Text-based Games

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper offers a mechanistic explanation for its LM results: pre-trained LMs supply linguistic/world/game priors that produce richer observation encodings, make reward cues more accessible, reduce reliance on learning language from scratch, and thereby improve action selection and some exploration; fine-tuning on gameplay transcripts adapts these priors to the domain.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory proposes a different mechanistic decomposition: structured memory provides compact relational, multi-hop reasoning and action-space pruning, episodic memory provides recall of detailed procedures and past successes/failures aiding long-horizon credit assignment, and their principled retrieval/integration into working memory is the causal mechanism producing robustness, sample efficiency, and generalization.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: That dual-memory causal account (distinct complementary roles for structured vs episodic memory plus a retrieval/integration policy) is not proposed, derived, or evaluated in the paper; the paper's mechanistic story concerns LM priors rather than an explicit structured↔episodic retrieval-mediated mechanism.

unification.paper_title: AriGraph: Learning Knowledge Graph World Models with Episodic Memory for LLM Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper explicitly unifies semantic (knowledge-graph) and episodic memories into a single world-model formalism G = (V_s, E_s, V_e, E_e) and motivates this with cognitive-memory analogies and algorithmic integration (joint retrieval feeding a single working memory).
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory frames multiple prior memory approaches (structured KG, episodic logs, unstructured RAG/full-history) as instances that can be subsumed by a hybrid integrated-memory principle explaining superior agent behavior.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: This unification is central to the paper: the authors present the combined graph abstraction, retrieval pipeline, and experiments showing that the integration (not either memory alone) drives performance; thus the unification claim is explicit and established in this work.

unification.paper_title: A Survey on Large Language Model-Based Game Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper explicitly unifies a broad literature under a reference architecture (memory, reasoning, perception–action), categorizes numerous agents by memory design (Table 3), and highlights common mechanisms (consolidation, structuring, retrieval) across environments and tasks.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory proposes a unifying principle that many disparate agent designs and observed benefits can be seen as instances of a single 'hybrid memory synergy' framework where joint structured+episodic memory yields superior robustness and generalization.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: While the survey provides a clear high-level unification and catalogs memory modalities and uses, it does not elevate the particular hybrid structured+episodic memory claim as the central unifying thesis nor present it as an explicit organizing law; treating hybrid memory as the primary unifier is therefore a conceptual emphasis that goes beyond the paper's explicit framing.

unification.paper_title: Knowledge-enhanced Agents for Interactive Text Games

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: Prior work has applied KGs, affordances, and action-history in various isolated ways to text-game or embodied agents (KG-A2C, DRRN variants, LM history inputs, etc.), but these were typically studied within single architectures or single injection modalities.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The paper provides a single experimental framework that systematically injects the same two knowledge types (affordances and MCA) into multiple training paradigms (RL and LM), multiple architectures, and through multiple injection modalities, enabling direct cross-paradigm comparison.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: This cross-architecture, cross-injection comparative evaluation pragmatically unifies disparate prior approaches at the empirical level (showing common patterns and differences), but it stops short of a formal theoretical unification.

unification.paper_title: MemInsight: Autonomous Memory Augmentation for LLM Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper situates MemInsight relative to prior methods (RAG, MemoryBank, DPR, other memory pipelines) and shows attribute-based augmentations can complement embedding/RAG-style retrieval, but it treats these as distinct techniques rather than formally unifying them.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory explicitly proposes a unifying principle that structured (knowledge-graph/semantic) and episodic (trajectory/experience) memories are components of a single hybrid memory architecture whose integration explains superior robustness, sample-efficiency, and generalization.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: While the paper hints that structured augmentations complement unstructured history and RAG, it does not formalize or validate a domain-wide unification of diverse memory paradigms into a single hybrid principle; the unification claim is therefore not established by the paper.

unification.paper_title: A Mixture-of-Experts Approach to Few-Shot Task Transfer in Open-Ended Text Worlds

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper explicitly unifies two technical elements in a single architecture: knowledge-graph (structured) persistent state representations (KG-A2C family) and an MoE policy composition mechanism (frozen experts + trainable expert + attention), demonstrating that these can be combined for few-shot role transfer in text worlds.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory-level claim is a broader unification that structured relational memory and episodic trajectory memory are complementary instances of a single hybrid-memory principle whose joint retrieval systematically improves robustness and generalization for LLM-based agents in text games.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: That higher-level conceptual unification (structured + episodic memory as a single operative principle) is not made or empirically supported in the paper — the paper only unifies KG-based structured memory with MoE policy reuse, not an episodic memory component — so the theory's unification is not present in the paper.

unification.paper_title: Generative Agents: Interactive Simulacra of Human Behavior

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper explicitly unifies observation logging, retrieval, reflection (abstraction), and hierarchical planning into a single agent architecture and empirically shows the joint system outperforms ablations that remove one or more components, arguing that their combination produces long-term coherence and emergent social dynamics.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The Hybrid Memory Synergy Law explicitly unifies two distinct memory modalities—structured semantic/state representations and episodic reflective trajectories—into a single integrated retrieval-and-working-memory framework whose combined use is claimed to produce synergistic gains over either modality alone or over naïve unstructured full-history retrieval.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper contains an environment tree used for grounding and an episodic natural-language memory pipeline, but it does not present, formalize, or experimentally validate the specific conceptual unification of a semantic/graph-structured memory tightly integrated with an episodic reflective memory as a single retrievable substrate; that precise unification claim is not made or tested here.

unification.paper_title: Monte Carlo Planning with Large Language Model for Text-Based Game Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper situates MC-DML relative to prior threads (KG/state-structure methods, MCTS+RL hybrids, LLM-as-prior methods) and unifies short-term trajectory context and cross-trial reflection as two complementary memory types used inside an LLM prior for MCTS.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: It explicitly unifies in-trial (working) memory and cross-trial (episodic/reflection) memory within an MCTS+LLM framework, showing their combined benefit empirically.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The theory's stronger unification claim — that structured relational memory (e.g., knowledge graphs) and episodic/reflective memory are instances of a single hybrid memory whose principled joint retrieval yields the reported benefits — is not realized or tested in the paper; achieving that full unification would require integrating KG-style representations with the reflection mechanism.

unification.paper_title: Keep CALM and Explore: Language Models for Action Generation in Text-based Games

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper situates CALM relative to two distinct prior strands: (1) linguistic-prior/action-affordance approaches (n-grams, Word2Vec, seq2seq) and (2) structured-graph/knowledge-graph RL approaches (KG-A2C), and it empirically compares CALM to both kinds of baselines. It thus highlights complementary strengths: LMs provide linguistic priors while KGs provide structured state augmentation.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The Hybrid Memory Synergy Law explicitly asserts a unification: that structured knowledge-graph style state representations and episodic trajectory/reflective memories are instances of complementary components of a single hybrid memory architecture whose integration produces superior agent performance.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: Although the paper compares and contrasts LM-based and graph-based methods (so the idea of complementarity is implicitly present), it does not explicitly formulate or prove a unifying principle that subsumes both as parts of one hybrid memory framework nor empirically validate that their integration yields the claimed unified improvements.

unification.paper_title: RAP: Retrieval-Augmented Planning with Contextual Memory for Multimodal LLM Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper explicitly unifies retrieval-augmented episodic memory, planning, and multimodal perception into an operational pipeline (Memory, Reasoner, Retriever, Executor) and situates RAP relative to RAG, ReAct, Reflexion, and ExpeL, showing that retrieval+planning can work across textual and embodied tasks.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory asserts a higher-level unification: that structured relational memory and episodic instance memory are instances of a single hybrid memory abstraction whose joint, principled retrieval is the core principle driving robustness and generalization in LLM agents.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: While the paper's logs contain structured fields (task, plan) combined with episodic instances, it does not formalize or experimentally validate the proposed canonical unification of an explicit KG-style structured memory with episodic trajectories as a single memory abstraction.

unification.paper_title: Pre-trained Language Models as Prior Knowledge for Playing Text-based Games

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper situates itself relative to prior approaches that use explicit knowledge graphs, object-centric retrieval, and action-generation LMs and empirically shows that LM-derived representations can match or outperform several KG-based and other baselines on many games, suggesting representational priors from LMs can substitute for some KG functionality.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory explicitly asserts a unifying principle: structured (KG-like) approaches and episodic (trajectory/reflective) retrieval approaches are complementary and should be combined into a single hybrid memory framework whose integration explains superior agent performance across text-game tasks.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: While the paper compares LM-based and KG-based methods and cites both lines of work, it does not explicitly state or formalize a unification that both memory types are complementary and that their integration yields the combined benefit; such an explicit unification can be plausibly synthesized from literature but is not made or tested in the paper.

unification.paper_title: Learning Dynamic Belief Graphs to Generalize on Text-Based Games

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper integrates several existing components—dynamic graph extraction from text, continuous multi-relational belief representations, R-GCN encoding, transformer text encoders, attention-based aggregation, and reinforcement learning—into a single end-to-end agent (GATA) that learns to infer and use latent belief graphs for decision making.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory-level claim proposes a unification that structured (KG-like) memory and episodic (trajectory/experience) memory are complementary instances of a single hybrid-memory principle whose deliberate integration and retrieval drives robustness and generalization in LLM agents.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: While the paper concretely unifies graph-structured state estimation with policy learning, it does not explicitly articulate or demonstrate the broader unification that pairs structured memory with an independent episodic memory as a single necessary principle; that higher-level unification is not shown in the paper.

unification.paper_title: Large Language Models Are Neurosymbolic Reasoners

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper unifies several prior ideas—LLM prompting, constrained prompt formats, and adding symbolic modules into the action space—into an operational neurosymbolic agent for text-based games, showing this combination is effective on the benchmark tasks.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The Hybrid Memory Synergy Law claims an explicit unification: structured relational representations and episodic (trajectory/reflective) traces should be treated as components of a single, synergistic hybrid-memory principle that explains improved robustness, sample efficiency, and generalization of LLM agents.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper does not propose or formalize such a unifying hybrid-memory principle that subsumes structured-state representations and episodic traces; it neither implements combined structured+episodic memory nor frames prior results as instances of one underlying memory-principle. The unification claim is therefore absent from this work.

unification.paper_title: Graph Constrained Reinforcement Learning for Natural Language Action Spaces

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper explicitly unifies a knowledge-graph-based state representation with a template-based, graph-masked action generation pipeline and argues that their combination enables scalable exploration of huge natural-language action spaces.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory asserts a broader unifying principle that multiple prior approaches (structured relational state representations and episodic/unstructured trajectory memory) should be treated as complementary components of a single hybrid memory architecture whose integration yields robust, generalizable LLM agents in text-game domains.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper already unifies state structure and action templates, but it neither includes episodic memory components nor claims that structured and episodic memories are instances of one unified memory framework; this higher-level unification is not present in the paper.

unification.paper_title: Deciphering Digital Detectives: Understanding LLM Behaviors and Capabilities in Multi-Agent Mystery Games

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper situates its design relative to prior retrieval-augmented, self-consistency, and in-context learning work and treats MR, SR, SV as complementary components within a single architecture for Jubensha.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory-level claim is a unification: structured relational memory and episodic trajectory memory are instances of a single synergistic memory framework that explains and improves agent behavior across tasks.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper does not propose or demonstrate this conceptual unification between explicit structured (relational) memory and episodic memory; it evaluates only unstructured episodic retrieval plus refinement/verification, so the proposed unified principle is absent from the paper.

unification.paper_title: Positive Experience Reflection for Agents in Interactive Text Environments

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The literature contains separate lines on graph/structured approaches for text games and on episodic/self-reflective LLM agents; the paper cites both but implements and evaluates only the episodic/reflection/managed-memory approach (Sweet&Sour).
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory explicitly unifies structured (knowledge-graph-like) and episodic (trajectory/reflection) memories into a single principle asserting synergistic gains when both are purposefully integrated and retrieved.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper does not perform or evaluate this unification, nor present empirical or theoretical evidence that combining structured relational state representations with episodic reflections outperforms either alone; that unification claim is not addressed and is novel relative to this paper.

unification.paper_title: Behavior Cloned Transformers are Neurosymbolic Reasoners

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper unifies a practical technique—action-space injection of symbolic-module actions—with behavior cloning across multiple module types and tasks, showing a single operational method works for arithmetic, navigation, sorting, and KB queries in text games.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory asserts a higher-level unification: that structured relational memory and episodic trajectory/reflective memory are instances of a single, synergistic memory-augmentation framework whose joint integration explains improved agent robustness and generalization.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper does not formalize or argue this kind of conceptual unification between structured and episodic memory systems; thus the unification claim in the theory is not present in or supported by the paper.

unification.paper_title: Reasoning, Memorization, and Fine-Tuning Language Models for Non-Cooperative Games

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper explicitly unifies two operational ideas: tree-of-thought search and a multi-agent task-decomposition pipeline, and it couples online agent interactions with an offline fine-tuning loop to improve performance. These elements are presented and experimentally evaluated together in a single pipeline.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory explicitly unifies two memory paradigms—structured relational state representations and episodic trajectory/experience memory—into a single claim that their integrated retrieval is the key principle giving robust, generalizable agent behavior.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper does not perform a unifying synthesis of structured versus episodic memory paradigms nor run controlled comparisons that treat those two memory types as components of a single framework; the specific unification of structured + episodic memory is therefore not present and represents a genuine novelty relative to this paper.

unification.paper_title: Reflexion: Language Agents with Verbal Reinforcement Learning

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper unifies several prior ideas into a single framework by formalizing an Actor/Evaluator/Self-Reflection loop and distinguishing short-term trajectory history from long-term textual reflections as memory components.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory explicitly asserts a unifying principle that structured (semantic/graph) memories and episodic (trajectory/reflective) memories are instances of a single hybrid memory architecture whose combined operation explains improved robustness and generalization.
