theory_law_dict.theory_name: Hybrid Structured and Episodic Memory Enables Robust, Generalizable LLM Agent Performance in Text Games
theory_law_dict.theory_description: Language model agents for text-based games achieve superior robustness, sample efficiency, and generalization when augmented with hybrid memory systems that combine explicit structured (e.g., knowledge-graph or belief-graph) state representations and episodic (trajectory or reflection-based) memory. Structured memory provides compact, relational representations that support multi-hop reasoning, navigation, and action-space pruning, while episodic memory enables recall of detailed instructions, procedures, and successful/failed experiences. The synergistic integration and principled retrieval of both kinds of memories enables agents to overcome both partial observability and long-horizon credit assignment bottlenecks, outperforming agents with either memory alone, with unstructured memory (e.g., full text history), or with naive retrieval/summarization.
theory_law_dict.theory_statement: When both structured (e.g., knowledge-graph/semantic) and episodic (e.g., observation/trajectory/reflective) memory are available and purposefully integrated and retrieved into agent working memory, LLM-based agents achieve higher normalized/game performance, reduced error rates, and greater sample efficiency and generalization compared to agents using only one memory type or only unstructured full-history/retrieval.
theory_law_dict.domain_scope: Applies to LLM agents in text-based games with partial observability, combinatorial action spaces, and complex state transitions, especially in environments requiring multi-step planning, navigation, and long-term instruction following (e.g., TextWorld, NetHack, general text-based tasks)
special_cases: Games solvable via short-term context or trivial observation-to-action mapping may show little benefit.
If memory construction (e.g., triplet extraction) is highly unreliable, benefits are reduced/disrupted.

aggregated_results.paper_title: AriGraph: Learning Knowledge Graph World Models with Episodic Memory for LLM Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper introduces a formal abstraction of memory as an integrated graph world model G=(V_s,E_s,V_e,E_e), episodic edges connecting semantic triplets to observations, and a two-stage retrieval + working-memory pipeline that separates planning and decision making.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory reframes agent memory design as a principled hybrid of structured (relational) and episodic (trajectory/observation) stores whose purposeful integration into working memory is the organizing principle for robust LLM agent cognition in text games.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: That conceptual reframing and the specific graph abstraction are the central methodological contribution of the paper and are explicitly developed and used; the theory's abstraction aligns directly with and is established by the paper.

aggregated_results.paper_title: A Survey on Large Language Model-Based Game Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The survey advances a clear conceptual architecture for LLMGAs (memory, reasoning, perception–action), and enumerates memory abstractions (working vs long-term, chunks/KV/trees/graphs/parametric) and associated operations (consolidation, structuring, retrieval).
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory reframes agent design by centering a hybrid structured+episodic memory system with principled retrieval into working memory as the key organizing abstraction for robust, generalizable LLM agent behavior in text games.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper already provides the canonical architecture and catalog of memory abstractions; centering the hybrid structured+episodic integration as the singular organizing lens is therefore an incremental reframing rather than a wholly new conceptual abstraction relative to the survey's explicit content.

aggregated_results.paper_title: Knowledge-enhanced Agents for Interactive Text Games

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper frames external knowledge for interactive agents along two axes—structured affordance knowledge (KG-style) and episodic action-memory (MCA)—and along injection modalities (KG triples, concatenation, separate encoders, pretraining), presenting an organizing taxonomy for knowledge injection experiments.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: This concise taxonomy (knowledge type × injection strategy × agent class) is made explicit and used to guide experimental comparisons and practical recommendations.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The framing is a useful, practical abstraction that organizes prior disparate techniques into a comparative experimental lens, but it is an incremental conceptual organization rather than a fundamentally new theoretical abstraction.

aggregated_results.paper_title: Evaluating and Enhancing LLMs Agent based on Theory of Mind in Guandan (搜蛋): A Multi-Player Cooperative Game under Imperfect Information

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper proposes a modular conceptualization for LLM-based embodied agents in imperfect-information games (State Interpreter, ToM Planner, Plan Evaluator) and frames planning in orders of ToM (zero/first/second order) as distinct algorithmic levels. It treats external tool invocation (action recommender) as a first-class module integrated into planning.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The hybrid-memory theory reframes agent memory as an explicit hybrid of structured (knowledge-graph/semantic) and episodic (trajectory/reflective) stores whose principled retrieval synergizes to solve partial observability and long-horizon credit assignment. The paper does not adopt that specific memory abstraction, but does introduce an abstraction of ToM-order planning and tool-augmented modular agents.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper's conceptual contribution is the ToM-order abstraction and modular pipeline for integrating belief inference and external tools into LLM decision loops; this reframing is explicitly articulated and evaluated in the paper but is distinct from the hybrid structured/episodic-memory abstraction in the theory statement.

aggregated_results.paper_title: Graph Constrained Reinforcement Learning for Natural Language Action Spaces

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper advances a conceptual reframing for IF agents: represent partially observed textual worlds as structured knowledge graphs (triples), use GATs to embed that structure, and treat language actions as template-structured generation constrained by a graph mask that binds state to generation.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory generalizes this into an abstract hybrid-memory architecture that organizes agent memory into complementary structured (relational) and episodic (trajectory/reflective) stores with principled retrieval into working memory for decision-making, especially for LLM-based agents.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The hybrid-memory abstraction is a natural conceptual generalization of the paper's structured-state idea but is not explicitly proposed or instantiated in the paper; therefore the reframing is a derivable but unstated conceptual extension of the paper's contributions.

aggregated_results.paper_title: MemInsight: Autonomous Memory Augmentation for LLM Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper presents an organizing abstraction for agent memory: memory entries augmented with attribute–value pairs, organized by perspective (entity vs. conversation), granularity (turn vs. session), and prioritized annotations feeding refined vs. comprehensive retrieval strategies.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory reframes agent memory design as a broader dichotomy between structured (semantic/relational) and episodic (trajectory/experiential) memories and prescribes their synergistic, principled retrieval into working memory as a general architectural principle for robust agents in partially observable, long-horizon text environments.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: MemInsight already offers a new attribute-centric abstraction for structured memory, but it does not formalize or center the structured-vs-episodic dichotomy as a general architectural law for interactive text-game agents; that higher-level reframing is an extension beyond the paper's framing.

aggregated_results.paper_title: Generative Agents: Interactive Simulacra of Human Behavior

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper introduces and formalizes new abstractions for agent memory and cognition: a natural-language memory stream of timestamped observations, periodic reflections (recursive synthesis of memories into higher-level insights), plans-as-memories with hierarchical decomposition, and an environment tree for grounding; it frames agent coherence as arising from storage, retrieval, reflection, and planning.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The Hybrid Memory Synergy Law reframes agent memory architecture as an explicit dual-modality abstraction (structured semantic/graph state + episodic trajectory/reflective memory) and centers principled joint retrieval into working memory as the organizing principle for robust, generalizable LLM-agent performance in partial-observability, long-horizon tasks.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper contributes a substantial conceptual reframing (memory stream + reflections + planning) but does not present the dual-modality (semantic KG + episodic traces) abstraction or treat a semantic knowledge-graph as a central retrievable memory modality integrated with episodic traces; that particular abstraction and emphasis on KG+episodic integration is not developed in the paper.

aggregated_results.paper_title: Monte Carlo Planning with Large Language Model for Text-Based Game Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper introduces and uses a concise conceptual abstraction partitioning memory for LLM agents into in-trial (short trajectory window / working memory) and cross-trial (episodic reflection) components and frames MC-DML as simulating human-like retention and reflection within MCTS planning.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: It formalizes how those two memory abstractions are encoded, retrieved, and injected into LLM prompts to form a dynamic prior inside PUCT selection.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The broader conceptual reframing proposed by the theory — treating structured relational state representations and episodic traces as a single hybrid memory abstraction with principled retrieval algorithms — is not adopted or operationalized in the paper and thus remains a conceptual extension beyond the paper's contribution.

aggregated_results.paper_title: RAP: Retrieval-Augmented Planning with Contextual Memory for Multimodal LLM Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper provides an operational abstraction of memory as structured logs (task, overall plan, trajectory) and describes a modular pipeline (Memory, Reasoner, Retriever, Executor) that reframes episodic retrieval as supplying in‑context learning exemplars to the agent.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory reframes agent memory conceptually as a two-component hybrid abstraction (structured relational memory + episodic instance memory) with principled joint retrieval and integration as the organizing lens for agent cognition in sequential, partially observable tasks.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The explicit conceptual reframing to a canonical hybrid KG+episodic abstraction and the prescription to treat joint retrieval as the primary organizing abstraction is not formalized or advocated in the paper, although some of the paper's design choices (task/plan fields) hint toward mixing structured cues with episodic instances.

aggregated_results.paper_title: Learning Dynamic Belief Graphs to Generalize on Text-Based Games

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper reframes learned latent belief graphs as an inductive-bias memory mechanism for partially observable text games: it treats a dynamic adjacency tensor as the agent’s structured memory and formalizes graph construction and update as a learned memory operation used during planning and action selection.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory elevates this into a broader abstraction: a hybrid-memory design pattern that prescribes combining explicit relational (KG-like) representations with episodic (trajectory/experience) memory and principled retrieval into working memory for LLM agents.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper embodies and defends the conceptual reframing of graphs-as-memory, but it does not formalize or evaluate the proposed hybrid-memory abstraction combining separate episodic stores with structured graphs; that explicit abstraction is an extension derivable from but not present in the paper.

aggregated_results.paper_title: Large Language Models Are Neurosymbolic Reasoners

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper reframes LLM agents that call external symbolic modules as 'neurosymbolic reasoners' and provides an operational abstraction (role initialization, action-query prompting, constrained prompts) for integrating tools with LLMs in interactive text-game agents.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The Hybrid Memory Synergy Law proposes a different conceptual abstraction: treating agent cognition as an interacting hybrid memory system (structured relational memory + episodic memory) whose principled retrieval into working memory is the core organizing principle for robust, generalizable agent behavior.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper does not introduce or adopt this hybrid memory abstraction — it focuses on prompting and tool integration rather than designing or framing agents around explicit structured and episodic memory subsystems — so this reframing is novel with respect to the paper.

aggregated_results.paper_title: Deciphering Digital Detectives: Understanding LLM Behaviors and Capabilities in Multi-Agent Mystery Games

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper frames improvements in terms of concrete modules (Memory Retriever, Self-Refinement, Self-Verification) and practical engineering choices, but it does not articulate a high-level taxonomy that separates and prescribes structured versus episodic memory as the central design principle.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory reframes agent memory design as an abstraction that separates structured relational memory and episodic trajectory/reflective memory and prescribes their purposeful integration and retrieval as the organizing principle for robust, generalizable LLM agents in text games.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: This conceptual reframing (explicit structured-vs-episodic decomposition and its primacy as a design law) is not present in the paper, which remains at the level of unstructured episodic retrieval plus iterative refinement; thus the abstraction is new relative to this work.

aggregated_results.paper_title: Positive Experience Reflection for Agents in Interactive Text Environments

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper reframes reflection as a managed-memory design (short-term and long-term reflection buffers) and introduces the conceptual distinction of reflecting on positive versus negative experiences as an architectural choice for LLM agents.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory reframes agent memory architecture at a higher abstraction: a hybrid two-component memory (structured relational state + episodic trajectory/reflection store) whose principled integration and retrieval policy is central to robust, generalizable agent behaviour.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper advances the managed-episodic reflection abstraction but does not propose or analyze the higher-level hybrid structured+episodic memory abstraction or its retrieval policies; that reframing is not present in the paper.

aggregated_results.paper_title: Behavior Cloned Transformers are Neurosymbolic Reasoners

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper frames the contribution operationally as neurosymbolic action-space injection—treating symbolic modules as callable actions made available to sequence models—rather than as an explicit memory-systems abstraction.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory reframes LLM-agent augmentation as the principled integration of two complementary memory abstractions—structured relational memory and episodic trajectory/reflective memory—centered on retrieval policies and working-memory composition as the core design principle for robust generalization.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: This conceptual reframing (treating symbolic/tool augmentation and trajectory data as distinct memory systems whose joint retrieval is the key architectural primitive) is not presented in the paper and thus constitutes a novel abstraction relative to the work.

aggregated_results.paper_title: A Mixture-of-Experts Approach to Few-Shot Task Transfer in Open-Ended Text Worlds

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper operationalizes persistent structured state as a knowledge graph (KG) and treats pre-trained, story-shaped KG-A2C agents as modular, reusable experts assembled via an attention-based MoE for transfer; it therefore adopts a modular policy/memory engineering view within KG-A2C frameworks.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory reframes agent design at a higher abstraction: explicitly positing a canonical hybrid-memory architecture (structured relational memory + episodic trajectory/reflection memory) whose principled retrieval into working memory is the organizing design principle for robust, generalizable LLM agents in text games.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: This explicit hybrid-memory conceptual reframing is not present in the paper (which lacks an episodic-memory substrate or the associated abstract framing), so the reframing is a novel conceptual contribution beyond what the paper presents.

aggregated_results.paper_title: Reasoning, Memorization, and Fine-Tuning Language Models for Non-Cooperative Games

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper reframes LLM game solving as a multi-agent system of specialized language-model agents that exchange compact, consistent text-based representations (summaries/areas) via a tree-of-thought interaction pattern; this is presented as an organizing abstraction for reducing memory growth and improving reasoning.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory proposes a different abstraction: modeling agent memory as two orthogonal, complementary systems (structured relational state stores and episodic trajectory/experience stores) with principled retrieval into a working memory as the central organizing design for robust LLM agents.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The two-tier memory abstraction (structured + episodic) and its centrality to agent design are not introduced or operationalized in the paper; this conceptual reframing is therefore novel relative to the paper's contributions.

aggregated_results.paper_title: Reflexion: Language Agents with Verbal Reinforcement Learning

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: Reflexion reframes policy improvement for language agents as 'verbal reinforcement learning' and gives an abstraction separating Actor, Evaluator, and Self-Reflection plus a short-term (trajectory) vs long-term (textual reflections) memory decomposition.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory offers a different conceptual abstraction: treating the agent memory substrate as a hybrid composed of explicit structured semantic/graph state representations jointly with episodic reflective traces and emphasizing principled retrieval/integration into working memory as the organizing principle for robust LLM agents.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: While Reflexion introduces a verbal-reinforcement abstraction and episodic vs short-term decomposition, it does not propose the specific hybrid structured+episodic memory abstraction or its retrieval-centered organization; that reframing is not advanced in the paper and is therefore novel relative to Reflexion.

aggregated_results.paper_title: Learning Knowledge Graph-based World Models of Textual Environments

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper introduces novel abstractions: casting KG generation as predicting graph differences and framing both graph diffs and valid actions as a Set-of-Sequences generation problem; it also conceptually treats mapping and acting as a joint, correlated problem solved by multi-task learning.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory reframes agent memory architecture at a higher level: it posits a principled hybrid-memory abstraction where structured relational state and episodic trajectory/reflective memory are distinct but integrable subsystems whose coordinated retrieval into working memory is the primary organizing principle for robust LLM agent behavior.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: That particular conceptual reframing (structured vs episodic memory as the canonical complementary abstraction for LLM agents, with principled retrieval integration) is not present in the paper and represents a new organizing abstraction beyond the paper's Set-of-Sequences and graph-diff reframings.

aggregated_results.paper_title: Language Models are Few-Shot Butlers

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper's organizing abstraction is 'action modeling' for language models plus the iterated action modeling loop (imitation → interaction → fine-tune); it does not adopt an explicit memory-systems taxonomy separating structured vs episodic memory as its core lens.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory reframes agent design through a memory-systems abstraction that explicitly distinguishes structured relational state representations from episodic trajectory/reflective memory and emphasizes principled retrieval into working memory as the organizing design principle.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: That conceptual reframing (treating LLM agents in text games as hybrid structured+episodic memory systems with retrieval policies as the central abstraction) is not present in the paper and represents a different organizing perspective.

aggregated_results.paper_title: Thespian: Multi-Character Text Role-Playing Game Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper offers a distinct conceptual reframing for text-RL by distinguishing 'character' agents from 'actor/thespian' agents, formalizing soft character prompts and per-character policy heads, and framing few-shot character composition as attention-based policy fusion.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory proposes a different abstraction: framing agent cognition and generalization in terms of a hybrid memory architecture composed of structured relational state and episodic trajectory/reflective stores with principled retrieval into working memory as the organizing lens.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The hybrid-memory abstraction and its use as the primary organizing concept for robust LLM-agent behavior is not present in the paper, so this conceptual reframing is novel relative to the paper's contributions.

aggregated_results.paper_title: diff History for Neural Language Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper introduces a concrete conceptual reframing: view interaction history not as raw text but as a task-agnostic, human-interpretable abstraction of relational text deltas (diffs) that alter how prompts are constructed and used for LM agents.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory proposes a higher-level conceptual abstraction decomposing agent memory into two complementary components—structured relational memory and episodic trajectory memory—and emphasizes principled retrieval and integration into working memory as an organizing design principle for robust LLM agents.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper promotes a practical abstraction (diffs) but does not adopt or argue for the broader conceptual decomposition of memory types with retrieval-integration policies; that higher-level reframing (structured vs episodic memory as the organizing abstraction) is not present in the paper and thus is novel relative to it.

aggregated_results.paper_title: Keep CALM and Explore: Language Models for Action Generation in Text-based Games

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: Conceptually, the paper reframes action generation in text games as a two-stage process: (1) produce a compact, context-conditioned candidate set via a language model (capturing linguistic priors) and (2) re-rank those candidates with RL to maximize reward. That reframing departs from purely rule-based or admissible-action handicaps.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The Hybrid Memory Synergy Law offers a different conceptual abstraction: treating agent memory as a hybrid system composed of structured relational state memory and episodic trajectory/reflective memory, with a principled retrieval and integration mechanism feeding working memory to LLM-based decision modules.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: Recasting agent memory as an explicitly hybrid structured+episodic memory system and proposing retrieval/integration as a central organizing abstraction for LLM agents is not present in the paper; the paper uses unstructured LM context windows and compares to graph-based baselines but does not adopt or formalize this hybrid-memory abstraction.

aggregated_results.paper_title: Pre-trained Language Models as Prior Knowledge for Playing Text-based Games

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper reframes the design choice for text-game agents by treating pre-trained LMs as a source of linguistic, world and game-sense priors that can replace or complement engineered knowledge graphs and that should be integrated into RL agents as a representation module.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory proposes a different organizing abstraction: architect agent memory as an explicitly hybrid system (structured relational memory + episodic trajectory/reflective memory) with principled retrieval into working memory as the central design principle for robust, generalizable LLM-agent behavior.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The hybrid structured-vs-episodic memory abstraction and its promotion as the primary organizing lens is not developed or instantiated in the paper; the paper's LM-as-prior framing is related but does not instantiate this specific abstraction, so the reframing is novel relative to the paper.

novelty_evaluation.paper_title: Aggregated Assessment: Hybrid Structured and Episodic Memory Enables Robust, Generalizable LLM Agent Performance

phenomenon_effect.what_is_known: A subset of papers (AriGraph, Knowledge-enhanced Agents, Learning Dynamic Belief Graphs, Positive Experience Reflection, MoE, and others) have explicitly demonstrated through controlled experiments that augmenting LLM agents with both structured memory (knowledge graphs, belief graphs, affordances) and episodic memory (trajectory logs, reflections, action histories) yields measurably higher performance metrics (normalized scores, success rates, sample efficiency) compared to single-memory or unstructured baselines across multiple text-game benchmarks (TextWorld, ScienceWorld, NetHack, Jericho, LIGHT). These demonstrations include quantitative comparisons and ablations isolating memory-component contributions.
phenomenon_effect.what_introduced: The theory statement formalizes and names a specific empirical regularity as the 'Hybrid Memory Synergy Law': that purposeful integration and retrieval of both structured (knowledge-graph/semantic) and episodic (observation/trajectory/reflective) memory into agent working memory systematically produces superior performance, reduced error rates, and enhanced sample efficiency and generalization in partially observable text games with combinatorial action spaces and long-horizon requirements, with specified boundary conditions (minimal benefit in short-context games; disruption when memory construction is unreliable).
phenomenon_effect.what_novel: While 6 papers (~27%) explicitly demonstrate this hybrid-memory performance benefit with experimental evidence, 11 papers (50%) do not test or measure this architecture at all, and comprehensive surveys classify the general principle as derivable rather than established. The phenomenon has been repeatedly demonstrated in specific implementations but has not been widely recognized, named, or treated as established knowledge across the field. The theory's contribution is elevating scattered empirical findings into an explicit, named law with formal scope and boundary conditions, rather than introducing a genuinely new empirical observation.

novelty_evaluation.paper_title: Aggregated Assessment: Hybrid Structured and Episodic Memory Enables Robust, Generalizable LLM Agent Performance

phenomenon_effect.what_is_known: Multiple papers (AriGraph, Knowledge-enhanced Agents, Learning Dynamic Belief Graphs, and others) have explicitly demonstrated through controlled experiments and ablations that augmenting LLM agents with combinations of structured memory (knowledge graphs, belief graphs, affordances) and episodic memory (trajectory logs, reflections, correct-action histories) yields measurably higher normalized game scores, success rates, and sample efficiency in text-based environments compared to baselines using only one memory type or unstructured full-history approaches. These findings span multiple benchmarks (TextWorld, ScienceWorld, NetHack, Jericho) and are supported by quantitative metrics and systematic ablations isolating memory-component contributions.
phenomenon_effect.what_introduced: The theory statement formalizes and generalizes a cross-study empirical regularity: that the purposeful integration and retrieval of both structured (knowledge-graph/semantic) and episodic (observation/trajectory/reflective) memory into agent working memory systematically produces superior performance, reduced error rates, and enhanced sample efficiency and generalization. It names this phenomenon the 'Hybrid Memory Synergy Law' and specifies the scope (partially observable text games with combinatorial action spaces and long-horizon requirements) and boundary conditions (games solvable via short-term context show minimal benefit; unreliable memory construction disrupts gains).
phenomenon_effect.what_novel: The specific empirical phenomenon—that hybrid structured+episodic memory integration outperforms single-memory-type or unstructured approaches—is explicitly established across multiple independent papers with direct experimental evidence, quantitative comparisons, and ablations. While operationalizations vary (KG vs. belief-graph, trajectory vs. reflection), the core performance benefit is repeatedly demonstrated. The phenomenon itself is therefore not novel; what the theory contributes is an explicit naming, formal statement, and cross-study synthesis of an already-documented empirical regularity. Some papers (11/22) do not test this hybrid architecture, and some (5/22) show related but not identical effects, but the core claim is empirically grounded in multiple explicit demonstrations.

These demonstrations include quantitative comparisons and ablations isolating memory-component contributions.

The phenomenon has been repeatedly demonstrated in specific implementations but has not been widely recognized, named, or treated as established knowledge across the field. The theory's contribution is elevating scattered empirical findings into an explicit, named law with formal scope and boundary conditions, rather than introducing a genuinely new empirical observation.

theory_law_dict.theory_name: Hybrid Structured and Episodic Memory Enables Robust, Generalizable LLM Agent Performance in Text Games
theory_law_dict.theory_description: Language model agents for text-based games achieve superior robustness, sample efficiency, and generalization when augmented with hybrid memory systems that combine explicit structured (e.g., knowledge-graph or belief-graph) state representations and episodic (trajectory or reflection-based) memory. Structured memory provides compact, relational representations that support multi-hop reasoning, navigation, and action-space pruning, while episodic memory enables recall of detailed instructions, procedures, and successful/failed experiences. The synergistic integration and principled retrieval of both kinds of memories enables agents to overcome both partial observability and long-horizon credit assignment bottlenecks, outperforming agents with either memory alone, with unstructured memory (e.g., full text history), or with naive retrieval/summarization.
theory_law_dict.theory_statement: When both structured (e.g., knowledge-graph/semantic) and episodic (e.g., observation/trajectory/reflective) memory are available and purposefully integrated and retrieved into agent working memory, LLM-based agents achieve higher normalized/game performance, reduced error rates, and greater sample efficiency and generalization compared to agents using only one memory type or only unstructured full-history/retrieval.
theory_law_dict.domain_scope: Applies to LLM agents in text-based games with partial observability, combinatorial action spaces, and complex state transitions, especially in environments requiring multi-step planning, navigation, and long-term instruction following (e.g., TextWorld, NetHack, general text-based tasks)
special_cases: Games solvable via short-term context or trivial observation-to-action mapping may show little benefit.
If memory construction (e.g., triplet extraction) is highly unreliable, benefits are reduced/disrupted.

aggregated_results.paper_title: AriGraph: Learning Knowledge Graph World Models with Episodic Memory for LLM Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper empirically shows that an LLM agent (Ariadne) augmented with a hybrid memory (AriGraph: semantic knowledge graph + episodic vertices/edges) attains substantially higher normalized scores and succeeds on long-horizon text-game tasks where baselines (full history, summarization, RAG, Reflexion, Simulacra, and several RL agents) largely fail. Ablations (w/o episodic, w/o exploration) and comparisons across multiple tasks (Treasure Hunt, Cleaning, Cooking, NetHack) demonstrate task-dependent contributions of components.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory statement asserts a general empirical regularity: purposefully integrating structured semantic memory and episodic memory into working memory yields higher performance, lower errors, and improved sample efficiency/generalization for LLM agents in text games compared to single-type or unstructured-memory agents.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper already states and experimentally supports this exact phenomenon across multiple benchmarks and ablations; thus the phenomenon claimed by the theory is explicitly established in the paper.

aggregated_results.paper_title: Deciphering Digital Detectives: Understanding LLM Behaviors and Capabilities in Multi-Agent Mystery Games

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper empirically demonstrates that adding an unstructured retrieval memory (vector DB) plus Self-Refinement (SR) and Self-Verification (SV) modules increases factual and inferential question accuracy, raises similarity between chat history and full scripts, and improves civilian win-rate and murderer-identification accuracy in the Jubensha multi-agent setting.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: It introduces and documents the empirical regularity that retrieval-augmented agents with iterative refinement and verification produce more authentic, informative responses and better task outcomes than memoryless baselines in this domain.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The specific phenomenon — that an unstructured episodic retrieval buffer combined with SR and SV yields measurable performance gains in Jubensha — is an explicit experimental finding of this paper and presented as a primary result.

aggregated_results.paper_title: Knowledge-enhanced Agents for Interactive Text Games

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper empirically demonstrates that injecting two external knowledge types—structured object affordances (from ConceptNet/KBs) and short-term episodic memory of previous correct actions (MCA)—into text-game agents improves task performance on many ScienceWorld tasks across four agent classes (DRRN, KG-A2C, RoBERTa, Swift). The experiments report aggregate and per-task improvements and show that affordances tend to produce larger gains than MCA and that combining both sometimes yields the best results.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The paper operationalizes and measures the performance effect of structured (affordance) and episodic (MCA) memory injections, both separately and combined, across multiple architectures and ten diverse ScienceWorld tasks, quantifying where each knowledge type helps or hurts.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The empirical claim that structured affordances plus episodic correct-action memory produce measurable, often complementary, performance gains (and that affordances are generally more effective than MCA on these benchmarks) is explicitly established in this paper for the tested models and tasks.

aggregated_results.paper_title: Positive Experience Reflection for Agents in Interactive Text Environments

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: Prior work focused on self-reflection from failures and storing reflections in long-term memory (e.g., Reflexion). The paper experimentally demonstrates that adding positive-experience reflection plus a managed short-/long-term reflection buffer (Sweet&Sour) improves average success scores on the ScienceWorld benchmark across three LLM families.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: Sweet&Sour introduces the empirical effect that explicitly reflecting on successful sub-goals (in addition to failures) and using managed memory increases task performance, reduces failure ‘tilt’, and yields proportionally larger gains for smaller LLMs on ScienceWorld.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The specific empirical phenomenon — consistent across tasks and model sizes within ScienceWorld — that positive-experience reflections combined with a dual-buffer managed memory produce measurable performance and robustness improvements is established by this paper.

aggregated_results.paper_title: A Mixture-of-Experts Approach to Few-Shot Task Transfer in Open-Ended Text Worlds

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper empirically demonstrates that an attention-weighted Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) composed of multiple frozen, role-aligned KG-A2C experts plus a single trainable ('hot') expert obtains higher zero-shot performance, faster few-shot reward discovery (greater sample efficiency), and higher final scores on blended and partial-blend role tasks in a LIGHT-derived open-ended text environment than single-expert baselines or training from scratch.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: It introduces the empirical effect that combining frozen role experts with a learnable expert and value-seeded attention produces robust few-shot task transfer and specific robustness patterns (sensitivity to distractor/irrelevant experts, failure under adversarial removal).
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The phenomenon—the MoE synergy (frozen experts + hot expert + value seeding + attention) yielding improved few-shot transfer in KG-A2C text-role environments—is an explicit, central experimental finding of this paper and is established within the paper's domain and benchmarks.

aggregated_results.paper_title: Learning Dynamic Belief Graphs to Generalize on Text-Based Games

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: This paper provides empirical evidence that a learned, continuous multi-relational belief-graph (dynamic structured memory) derived from partial text observations improves normalized test performance and generalization across a large distribution of TextWorld games compared to strong text-only baselines. It also shows that access to exact ground-truth graphs yields an even higher performance upper bound. The effect is measured across hundreds of unique games and multiple difficulty levels.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The paper explicitly introduces and documents the phenomenon that learned graph-structured belief states act as an effective memory representation that materially improves policy learning and generalization in TextWorld. It names and operationalizes this effect via the GATA agent and ablations (OG/COC pretraining, discrete vs continuous updaters).
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The phenomenon (learned continuous belief graphs improving generalization in text-game families) is an explicit, central empirical contribution of the paper and is established by its experiments.

aggregated_results.paper_title: A Survey on Large Language Model-Based Game Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The survey documents that memory is a central component of LLM-based game agents, distinguishing working vs long-term memory and multiple storage formats (chunks, key–value, trees, graphs, parametric), and it reports agent-level examples where memory mechanisms (e.g., reflection, Last-Thoughts, external knowledge retrieval) improve decision consistency and task performance in particular environments.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The evaluated theory asserts a concrete empirical phenomenon: purposefully integrating structured (knowledge-graph/semantic) and episodic (trajectory/reflective) memories into working memory yields higher normalized/game performance, lower error rates, and greater sample efficiency and generalization than agents using only one memory type or only unstructured full-history retrieval.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper catalogs separate uses and benefits of structured and episodic memories and presents per-agent empirical cases, but it does not provide an explicit cross-study empirical claim or meta-analysis demonstrating that a hybrid structured+episodic integration systematically outperforms single-type or unstructured baselines across games; the specific general performance law is therefore not explicitly established in the survey.

aggregated_results.paper_title: MemInsight: Autonomous Memory Augmentation for LLM Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper demonstrates empirically that structured, attribute-based augmentations of LLM agent memory improve retrieval effectiveness and downstream task performance (QA, conversational recommendation, event summarization) relative to baselines using raw/unstructured history or standard RAG; improvements appear across multiple metrics (F1/recall and LLM-based subjective metrics).
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory statement claims a broader phenomenon: that a hybrid memory combining explicit structured representations and episodic (trajectory/experience) memory purposefully integrated into working memory yields higher task performance, robustness, sample efficiency, and generalization in LLM agents operating in text-based games and related partially observable, long-horizon tasks.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: Extending the paper's demonstrated benefit of structured augmentations in dialogue tasks to a general hybrid-memory phenomenon (structured + episodic) in interactive text-game environments is not established by the paper; the empirical results support structured-memory benefits but do not show the synergistic role of a distinct episodic subsystem in text games.

aggregated_results.paper_title: Learning Knowledge Graph-based World Models of Textual Environments

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper demonstrates empirically that using an explicit structured memory in the form of knowledge graphs (with a Worldformer that predicts graph differences) substantially improves knowledge-graph and valid-action prediction metrics over unstructured/extractive baselines on a suite of text games. It also shows that multi-task learning and an SOS loss further increase prediction accuracy and make the modeling problem more tractable. The work establishes that structured graph representations enable useful look-ahead (e.g., inferring chest contents) not available to simple extractive approaches.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory statement claims a distinct empirical phenomenon: LLM agents that combine structured (knowledge-graph) and episodic (trajectory/reflective) memory and purposefully integrate retrieval achieve higher gameplay-normalized performance, lower error rates, and greater sample efficiency/generalization than agents with only one memory type or only unstructured full-history retrieval.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The specific empirical effect — measurable performance and sample-efficiency gains from a hybrid structured+episodic memory architecture in LLM agents playing text games — is not reported or evaluated in the paper. The paper documents benefits of structured memory alone but does not test or quantify the proposed hybrid-memory phenomenon.

aggregated_results.paper_title: Generative Agents: Interactive Simulacra of Human Behavior

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper demonstrates empirically that an LLM-driven agent architecture that records natural-language observations (memory stream), synthesizes periodic higher-level reflections, and produces hierarchical plans yields substantially more believable, coherent, and socially emergent behavior than ablated variants lacking these components; this is shown via interview-based believability rankings (TrueSkill) and a 25-agent two-day sandbox deployment with measured information diffusion, relationship formation, and coordination.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The Hybrid Memory Synergy Law asserts a broader empirical regularity: agents that combine explicit structured (knowledge-graph/semantic) memory and episodic (observation/trajectory/reflection) memory with principled joint retrieval will achieve higher normalized/game performance, lower error rates, greater sample efficiency, and improved generalization than agents using only one memory type or only unstructured full-history retrieval.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper provides strong evidence for the benefit of episodic natural-language memory plus reflection and planning, but it does not evaluate or demonstrate the specific additional benefit claimed for integrating an explicit structured semantic/graph memory (e.g., multi-hop KG retrieval, action-space pruning) with episodic traces; that combined structured+episodic performance claim is not empirically shown here.

aggregated_results.paper_title: Monte Carlo Planning with Large Language Model for Text-Based Game Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper empirically shows that integrating an LLM prior with short in-trial trajectory context and cross-trial episodic reflections into MCTS (MC-DML) yields substantially higher initial planning performance and better handling of bottleneck states across nine Jericho text games compared to multiple RL, KG-based, and LLM/MCTS baselines; ablations demonstrate performance drops when either memory component is removed. Prior work has separately reported benefits of knowledge-graph state representations and of LLM-based reflection, but not this exact MC-DML configuration.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: It introduces the concrete empirical phenomenon that LLM-guided MCTS augmented with both an in-trial short trajectory window and cross-trial textual reflections produces large gains in initial (iteration-1) game performance and sample efficiency on the tested Jericho suite.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper documents a clear empirical effect for episodic (reflection) + short-term context memory in LLM+MCTS agents, but it does not provide empirical evidence for the specific hybrid phenomenon claimed by the theory that combines structured (KG-like) memory with episodic memory; that structured+episodic phenomenon is not demonstrated here.

aggregated_results.paper_title: RAP: Retrieval-Augmented Planning with Contextual Memory for Multimodal LLM Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper empirically demonstrates that storing and retrieving prior successful episodes (episodic trajectory logs) and presenting windowed trajectory examples as in‑context demonstrations improves agent success rates, reward scores, and sample efficiency across multiple textual and multimodal benchmarks (ALFWorld, WebShop, Franka Kitchen, Meta-World). It also shows that selective retrieval keys, adaptive similarity weighting, and windowing reduce noise and aid performance.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory statement claims a distinct phenomenon: a synergistic performance boost when an explicit structured (e.g., knowledge-graph/semantic) memory is combined with episodic (trajectory/experience) memory and jointly retrieved into working memory, outperforming episodic-only, structured-only, or unstructured full-history approaches.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper supports the empirical effect of episodic retrieval but does not implement, compare, or empirically validate an explicit structured relational memory (e.g., KG) nor the incremental benefit of a KG+episodic hybrid. Thus the specific empirical phenomenon (hybrid structured+episodic synergy) is not established in the paper.

aggregated_results.paper_title: Large Language Models Are Neurosymbolic Reasoners

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper empirically demonstrates that zero-shot prompted LLM agents (GPT-3.5/GPT-4) that can call external symbolic modules (calculator, navigator, sorter, knowledge-base) achieve high normalized game performance (average ≈0.88 on the tested benchmarks) and outperform or match RL and behavior-cloned baselines on several symbolic text-game tasks. It reports task-specific failure modes (e.g., forgetting navigation routes, limited internal memory for sorting) that affect agent efficiency and accuracy.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The Hybrid Memory Synergy Law asserts a distinct empirical effect: agents that purposefully integrate both structured (knowledge-graph/semantic) and episodic (trajectory/reflective) memories into working memory will reliably obtain higher normalized performance, lower error rates, and greater sample efficiency and generalization than agents with only one memory type or only unstructured full-history retrieval.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper does not present experiments or empirical measurements comparing combined structured+episodic memory augmentation to single-memory or unstructured-history baselines; it evaluates prompting plus external symbolic modules but not a hybrid structured/episodic memory architecture. Therefore the specific empirical phenomenon claimed by the Hybrid Memory Synergy Law is not observed or tested in this work.

aggregated_results.paper_title: Graph Constrained Reinforcement Learning for Natural Language Action Spaces

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper empirically demonstrates that a dynamically built structured memory (a knowledge graph) used as the agent state, combined with a template-based action space and graph-based masking, improves performance and exploration efficiency across a suite of 28 interactive-fiction games versus a strong template-based baseline (TDQN). Ablations show that graph-derived components accelerate learning and that removing template structure (full vocabulary decoding) degrades performance severely.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The submitted theory claims a distinct empirical phenomenon: that agents having both structured (knowledge-graph) and episodic (trajectory/reflective) memories purposefully integrated achieve synergistic gains in normalized/game performance, lower error rates, and greater sample efficiency and generalization compared to agents with only one memory type or only unstructured full-history retrieval.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper provides clear evidence for benefits of structured memory but contains no experiments or claims about episodic-memory stores or their integration with structured memory; therefore the specific hybrid-structured+episodic phenomenon asserted by the theory is not evidenced in this paper.

aggregated_results.paper_title: Behavior Cloned Transformers are Neurosymbolic Reasoners

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper demonstrates empirically that injecting actions from symbolic modules (arithmetic, navigation, sorting, knowledge-base lookup) into the action space yields large, consistent performance and efficiency gains for a behavior-cloned transformer across four text-game benchmarks, while the DRRN baseline does not benefit under the reported training regime.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The evaluated theory claims a named phenomenon: that purposeful joint availability and retrieval of structured (knowledge-graph/semantic) and episodic (trajectory/reflective) memory produces superior normalized/game performance, lower error rates, and improved sample efficiency and generalization relative to single-memory or unstructured-history agents.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper shows benefits of structured symbolic tool injection plus supervised trajectories but does not test or name episodic-memory retrieval/integration at inference; therefore the specific hybrid-memory phenomenon asserted by the theory (structured+episodic synergy at retrieval time) is not demonstrated in this work.

aggregated_results.paper_title: Reasoning, Memorization, and Fine-Tuning Language Models for Non-Cooperative Games

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper empirically demonstrates that a multi-agent, tree-of-thought decomposition (summarization, area selection, action selection, validation) yields higher win rates (~60–65%), lower error rates, and sample-efficient fine-tuning (~1k examples yields ≈10% improvement) on the introduced Goedendag game compared to direct single-model and CoT baselines. Ablations show removing representation/area-selection tasks degrades win rate and increases error rate. The paper thus establishes an empirical effect for this particular pipeline and game domain.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory statement claims a distinct phenomenon: that agents explicitly combining structured (knowledge-graph/semantic) memory and episodic (trajectory/experience) memory, with principled retrieval into working memory, outperform agents with only one memory type or with naive unstructured history retrieval.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper does not implement, compare, or measure separate structured versus episodic memory stores or their joint effect; it uses compact text-based representations and task decomposition but provides no experiments isolating an episodic memory component or hybrid-structured+episodic architectures. Therefore the specific phenomenon claimed by the theory (hybrid structured+episodic memory superiority) is not present in the paper and is novel relative to it.

aggregated_results.paper_title: Reflexion: Language Agents with Verbal Reinforcement Learning

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper empirically demonstrates that storing natural-language self-reflections (episodic textual memory) and conditioning subsequent trials on them yields large, consistent improvements across decision-making (AlfWorld), multi-hop reasoning (HotPotQA), and code-generation (HumanEval/MBPP/Leetcode) benchmarks, with quantitative gains and ablations isolating the self-reflection step.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The evaluated theory claims a distinct phenomenon: a synergistic performance boost when explicit structured (e.g., knowledge-graph/semantic) memory is combined with episodic (trajectory/reflective) memory and both are purposefully integrated and retrieved into working memory.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: Reflexion documents episodic/verbal-memory benefits but does not introduce, measure, or report any phenomenon involving combined structured+episodic memory; the hybrid-effect claim is absent from the paper's experiments and therefore novel relative to this paper.

aggregated_results.paper_title: Language Models are Few-Shot Butlers

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: This paper empirically demonstrates that pretrained autoregressive LMs fine-tuned on a small set of expert demonstrations and improved via iterated action modeling (self-generated successful trajectories) achieve large absolute gains in success rate and strong sample efficiency on the ALFWorld text environment, including some out-of-distribution human-annotated goals.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory statement asserts a specific empirical phenomenon: that combining structured (knowledge-graph/semantic) memory and episodic (trajectory/reflective) memory, with purposeful integration and retrieval into working memory, produces higher normalized/game performance, lower error rates, and better sample efficiency/generalization than using only one memory type or only unstructured full-history retrieval.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper provides evidence for gains from pretrained LMs and episodic-like replay (IAM) but does not construct, evaluate, or report results for explicit structured-memory representations or their hybrid integration with episodic memory; hence the hybrid structured+episodic phenomenon claimed by the theory is not observed in this work.

aggregated_results.paper_title: Thespian: Multi-Character Text Role-Playing Game Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper empirically demonstrates that a single thespian agent using soft character prompts and per-character action logits can match or exceed single-character baselines on character-specific objectives in the LIGHT environment, and that a frozen core plus a small attention fusion module enables fast few-shot learning of a blended character while preserving prior skills.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The submitted theory asserts a broader empirical phenomenon: that hybrid memory systems combining explicit structured (knowledge-graph) and episodic (trajectory/reflection) memory, with principled retrieval into working memory, yield superior robustness, sample efficiency, and generalization versus single-memory or unstructured full-history approaches in text-game LLM agents.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper reports improved multi-character policy fusion via prompt-conditioning and attention, but it does not present experiments or measurements that demonstrate the specific phenomenon of combined structured+episodic memory providing the claimed synergy; the hybrid-memory empirical claim is not established here.

aggregated_results.paper_title: diff History for Neural Language Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper provides strong empirical evidence that replacing full-text observation histories with Unix-style text deltas ('diff history') yields large improvements in sample efficiency, context-token compression (in high-dimensional settings), and downstream agent performance in procedurally-generated text environments (notably matching neural-agent SOTA on NetHack with far fewer labels). It reports ablations showing severe performance drops when diffs are removed and quantifies effects across BabyAI-Text and NetHack.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory statement claims a different empirical phenomenon: that purposefully integrating both explicit structured (knowledge/graph) memory and episodic (trajectory/reflective) memory into LLM agent working memory produces superior normalized/game performance, lower error rates, greater sample efficiency, and better generalization than using either memory type alone or unstructured full-history retrieval.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper documents that a particular form of structured episodic encoding (text diffs) improves agent performance, but it does not measure or compare explicit graph-like structured memory, nor tests combined structured+episodic architectures. Therefore the specific phenomenon that a hybrid structured+episodic memory system outperforms single-memory or naive full-history agents is not demonstrated in the paper and remains a new empirical claim relative to its content.

aggregated_results.paper_title: Keep CALM and Explore: Language Models for Action Generation in Text-based Games

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: This paper empirically demonstrates that a pretrained language model (CALM) trained on diverse human gameplay, when used to generate top-k action candidates and combined with a DRRN re-ranker, yields substantially better average normalized game score (≈69% relative improvement over the NAIL baseline) and can in many cases match or outperform agents that use ground-truth admissible-action handicaps. It also shows empirical regularities about quality–quantity tradeoffs (top-k affects precision/recall of admissible and gold actions) and that pretraining plus ClubFloyd fine-tuning are important. The paper documents that LM priors can produce many admissible and contextually-relevant actions across unseen games.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The Hybrid Memory Synergy Law asserts a new empirical phenomenon: purposefully integrating both structured (knowledge-graph) and episodic (trajectory/reflective) memories into agent working memory yields higher normalized/game performance, lower error rates, and better sample efficiency and generalization than any single memory type or naive full-history retrieval.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The specific empirical effect that a principled, integrated hybrid of structured and episodic memories produces strictly superior and robust LLM-agent performance in text games is not claimed or demonstrated in this paper; the paper shows benefits of an unstructured LM+RL pipeline and reports results for a knowledge-graph based baseline, but never tests or documents the proposed hybrid-memory synergy as a phenomenon.

aggregated_results.paper_title: Pre-trained Language Models as Prior Knowledge for Playing Text-based Games

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper empirically demonstrates that augmenting DRRN/TDQN agents with a pre-trained transformer (DistilBERT) fine-tuned on human gameplay transcripts yields higher game scores, improved sample efficiency (fewer environment interactions than baselines), and modest cross-game transfer in Jericho text games; it reports SOTA on 4/14 games and higher average normalized score than several baselines. The observed effect is that a single LM-based representation improves semantic understanding and enables better action selection in partially observable text-game environments.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory claims a distinct empirical phenomenon: purposefully integrating both structured (knowledge-graph/semantic) memory and episodic (trajectory/reflective) memory into agent working memory produces systematically higher normalized/game performance, lower error rates, and better sample efficiency and generalization than using either memory type alone or only unstructured full-history retrieval.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper does not empirically test or report experiments that combine an explicit structured memory and an episodic memory with principled joint retrieval into working memory; therefore the specific hybrid structured+episodic performance effect asserted by the theory is not present in the paper and is a novel empirical claim relative to it.

empirical_synthesis_meta_regulariry.what_is_known: One paper (AriGraph) explicitly establishes the hybrid structured+episodic memory meta-regularity through comprehensive experiments across TextWorld, NetHack, and QA benchmarks with full ablations confirming synergistic contributions. Ten papers provide partial evidence by demonstrating consistent benefits of either structured memory alone (e.g., belief graphs, KG-constrained RL) or episodic memory alone (e.g., verbal reflections, trajectory retrieval) within their respective experimental suites, showing reproducible within-modality improvements. One paper (GATA) explicitly demonstrates structured memory benefits but discusses the full hybrid pattern only peripherally.
empirical_synthesis_meta_regulariry.what_introduced: The theory asserts a general cross-domain empirical meta-regularity: purposeful integration of both structured (knowledge-graph/semantic) and episodic (trajectory/reflective) memory into LLM agent working memory systematically yields superior normalized performance, reduced errors, and greater sample efficiency/generalization compared to single-memory-type, unstructured-history, or naive-retrieval agents across text-based games with partial observability and complex state transitions. This positions the hybrid synergy as a reproducible architectural design principle rather than a task-specific optimization.
empirical_synthesis_meta_regulariry.what_novel: The meta-regularity is explicitly validated in one comprehensive study (AriGraph) but has not achieved broad recognition or independent replication across the wider text-game agent literature: 10 of 21 papers demonstrate benefits of individual memory types from which the hybrid principle could be derived but do not state or test it, and 9 papers provide no empirical basis for the hybrid claim at all. While rigorously demonstrated in at least one major work, the principle is not yet treated as established or central organizing knowledge by the field, indicating it remains at the periphery of collective awareness despite strong empirical support in one paper.

Nine additional papers provide partial evidence: they demonstrate either structured memory benefits (e.g., belief graphs in GATA, KG-constrained RL) or episodic memory benefits (e.g., Reflexion's verbal reflections, RAP's retrieval) separately across various text-game domains, showing consistent within-modality improvements. Multiple papers aggregate results showing that individual memory augmentations (structured tools, episodic reflections, or LM priors) improve performance across multiple games or tasks within their respective experimental suites.

This claim positions the hybrid approach as a reproducible architectural principle rather than a task-specific optimization, predicting consistent advantages across diverse text-game environments requiring multi-step planning and long-horizon reasoning.

For 9 papers the hybrid meta-regularity is derivable from their results (they validate one memory type's benefits) but unstated as a synthesized principle. For 10 papers the full hybrid claim represents a genuinely new empirical hypothesis beyond their experimental scope. This positions the hybrid synergy as a reproducible architectural design principle rather than a task-specific optimization.
