theory_law_dict.theory_name: Hybrid Structured and Episodic Memory Enables Robust, Generalizable LLM Agent Performance in Text Games
theory_law_dict.theory_description: Language model agents for text-based games achieve superior robustness, sample efficiency, and generalization when augmented with hybrid memory systems that combine explicit structured (e.g., knowledge-graph or belief-graph) state representations and episodic (trajectory or reflection-based) memory. Structured memory provides compact, relational representations that support multi-hop reasoning, navigation, and action-space pruning, while episodic memory enables recall of detailed instructions, procedures, and successful/failed experiences. The synergistic integration and principled retrieval of both kinds of memories enables agents to overcome both partial observability and long-horizon credit assignment bottlenecks, outperforming agents with either memory alone, with unstructured memory (e.g., full text history), or with naive retrieval/summarization.
theory_law_dict.theory_statement: When both structured (e.g., knowledge-graph/semantic) and episodic (e.g., observation/trajectory/reflective) memory are available and purposefully integrated and retrieved into agent working memory, LLM-based agents achieve higher normalized/game performance, reduced error rates, and greater sample efficiency and generalization compared to agents using only one memory type or only unstructured full-history/retrieval.
theory_law_dict.domain_scope: Applies to LLM agents in text-based games with partial observability, combinatorial action spaces, and complex state transitions, especially in environments requiring multi-step planning, navigation, and long-term instruction following (e.g., TextWorld, NetHack, general text-based tasks)
special_cases: Games solvable via short-term context or trivial observation-to-action mapping may show little benefit.
If memory construction (e.g., triplet extraction) is highly unreliable, benefits are reduced/disrupted.

aggregated_results.paper_title: AriGraph: Learning Knowledge Graph World Models with Episodic Memory for LLM Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper aggregates experiments across multiple games, NetHack, ablations, QA benchmarks, and token-efficiency analyses to show a consistent pattern: the hybrid AriGraph outperforms alternative memory designs and scales to harder tasks while maintaining retrieval/compute trade-offs.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory asserts this meta-regularity as a general law: hybrid structured+episodic memory reliably improves normalized performance, reduces errors, and increases sample efficiency/generalization across relevant benchmarks.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper already provides this empirical synthesis and demonstrates the consistent superiority of the hybrid approach across evaluated settings; therefore the meta-regularity is explicitly established rather than novel relative to this work.

aggregated_results.paper_title: Learning Dynamic Belief Graphs to Generalize on Text-Based Games

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: Within this paper there is a clear empirical regularity: graph-structured belief representations (learned via OG/COC pretraining and used by GATA) consistently improve policy learning and test-time generalization across the TextWorld suites tested, and ground-truth graphs yield an even stronger systematic advantage.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory asserts a higher-level meta-regularity that deliberately combining structured and episodic memories yields robust, sample-efficient, and generalizable LLM agents across text-game settings; the paper provides empirical support for the structured-memory half but does not synthesize evidence for the hybrid (structured+episodic) meta-regularity.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The full hybrid-memory meta-regularity (structured+episodic synergy) is not evidenced or synthesized in the paper and thus represents a novel, peripheral extension relative to the paper’s demonstrated empirical pattern.

aggregated_results.paper_title: MemInsight: Autonomous Memory Augmentation for LLM Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper provides empirical evidence across three dialogue tasks and two datasets that attribute-based augmentations and priority strategies produce consistent improvements in retrieval metrics and several downstream evaluations, indicating a repeatable pattern within the evaluated dialogue domain.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory asserts a broader meta-regularity: hybrid structured+episodic memory reliably produces robustness, sample-efficiency, and generalization advantages across text-based game environments and tasks involving partial observability and long-horizon planning.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: While the paper documents a consistent effect of structured augmentations across dialogue tasks, it does not synthesize evidence across the distinct memory paradigms (structured vs episodic) nor across interactive game domains; the claimed cross-domain meta-regularity is therefore not empirically established by the paper.

aggregated_results.paper_title: Behavior Cloned Transformers are Neurosymbolic Reasoners

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper reports a clear empirical regularity: across 100 test games per benchmark, behavior-cloned transformers trained on gold trajectories gain large, consistent performance and efficiency improvements when symbolic-module actions are injected, while the DRRN baseline does not exhibit comparable gains under the same conditions.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory asserts a broader meta-regularity that joint availability and principled retrieval of structured and episodic memory yields robust, sample-efficient, and generalizable agent behavior across text-game tasks, outperforming single-memory or unstructured-history approaches.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper provides strong empirical evidence for one axis (structured symbolic tools + trajectory supervision) but does not empirically evaluate episodic-memory retrieval at inference or contrast against unstructured full-history retrieval; therefore the fuller meta-regularity about hybrid structured+episodic memory is not demonstrated and must be inferred or tested in future work.

aggregated_results.paper_title: Reflexion: Language Agents with Verbal Reinforcement Learning

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper synthesizes empirical evidence across several task families to conclude a meta-regularity: verbal self-reflection (episodic textual memory) consistently improves sample efficiency and task performance across decision-making, reasoning, and code generation, supported by ablations and model comparisons.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory posits a broader empirical regularity that hybrids of structured and episodic memory produce systematically better robustness, generalization, and sample efficiency than either memory alone or naive full-history retrieval across text-game-style partial-observability tasks.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: Reflexion provides cross-task synthesis for episodic/verbal memory benefits but contains no empirical synthesis showing the additional advantage of combining structured and episodic memories; the hybrid meta-regularity is therefore not demonstrated in the paper and remains a derivable hypothesis rather than an established empirical regularity.

aggregated_results.paper_title: Learning Knowledge Graph-based World Models of Textual Environments

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: Empirically, the paper synthesizes across multiple test games to show a regular pattern: structured knowledge-graph based world models (Worldformer) using graph-diff prediction, multi-task learning, and SOS loss outperform several strong baselines on KG and valid-action prediction metrics across 9 unseen games; ablations quantify the importance of each component.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory asserts a broader meta-regularity: across agents and environments, combining structured and episodic memories yields consistent improvements in robustness, sample efficiency, error rates, and generalization relative to single-memory or unstructured-history agents.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper's empirical synthesis supports the value of structured memory but does not provide cross-study or cross-architecture evidence about hybrid structured+episodic memory; the proposed universal meta-regularity for hybrid memories is therefore not established here and remains an extension beyond the paper's synthesized results.

aggregated_results.paper_title: Generative Agents: Interactive Simulacra of Human Behavior

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper synthesizes evidence internally across controlled ablations and an end-to-end multi-agent deployment to show a consistent pattern: inclusion of observation recording, reflection synthesis, and planning each add measurable believability and enable emergent social phenomena; statistical analysis (TrueSkill, Kruskal-Wallis, Dunn tests) supports these internal comparisons.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The Hybrid Memory Synergy Law asserts a broader meta-regularity across tasks and domains: that combining structured and episodic memories systematically improves normalized/game performance, error rates, sample efficiency, and generalization relative to single-modality or unstructured-memory baselines.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper provides an empirical synthesis across internal architectural ablations and emergent outcomes but does not establish the cross-domain meta-regularity for structured+episodic hybrids (nor synthesize evidence across multiple external studies or game domains); the law's cross-task meta-regularity is therefore not empirically supported here.

aggregated_results.paper_title: Monte Carlo Planning with Large Language Model for Text-Based Game Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: Empirically, the paper synthesizes results over nine games showing a consistent pattern: adding cross-trial reflections and short in-trial context to an LLM prior inside MCTS improves scores, addresses bottlenecks, and raises initial planning performance relative to several baselines; ablations confirm the importance of both memory components.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: It supplies an empirical meta-regularity confined to the Jericho suite that episodic reflections plus limited trajectory context systematically improve LLM-guided MCTS performance across diverse text-game challenges.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The theory posits a stronger meta-regularity that this hybrid (structured+episodic) approach will systematically outperform single-memory or unstructured-history agents across broader text-game domains; the paper supports only the episodic+in-trial part of that claim, so the full hybrid meta-regularity is not empirically established here.

aggregated_results.paper_title: Thespian: Multi-Character Text Role-Playing Game Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: Within its experiments the paper synthesizes an empirical regularity: freezing a pre-trained core and training a small attention-based fusion over per-character logits yields faster few-shot learning of a blended character and preserves prior behaviors, whereas unfrozen retraining is slower and causes forgetting.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory asserts a broader meta-regularity that hybrid structured+episodic memory consistently outperforms either memory alone and beats unstructured full-history retrieval across methods and environments.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper provides a single-system internal synthesis about policy fusion but does not aggregate cross-study evidence to support a general meta-regularity about hybrid memories; extrapolating the broader regularity from this paper would be premature, so the theory-level synthesis is not supported here.

aggregated_results.paper_title: Evaluating and Enhancing LLMs Agent based on Theory of Mind in Guandan (搜蛋): A Multi-Player Cooperative Game under Imperfect Information

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper runs a multi-model empirical study (several open-source and closed-source LLMs) and synthesizes results showing (a) ToM planning consistently improves average scores across most models and opponents, (b) action-recommender tooling markedly improves LLM performance in high-branching action settings, and (c) GPT-4 with ToM+tooling approaches but does not always surpass a SOTA RL agent.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The hybrid-memory statement predicts a broad empirical regularity that combining structured and episodic memories improves robustness, sample efficiency and generalization. The paper introduces a different empirical regularity: that belief/ToM prompting plus an external action-pruning tool produces consistent gains across diverse LLMs in a complex text-game domain.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper provides a new cross-model empirical synthesis establishing that ToM-style belief inference and a lightweight external recommender together yield consistent improvements in a multi-agent imperfect-information card game; this is an empirical regularity relevant to augmentation strategies but does not test or confirm the hybrid structured+episodic memory synergy the theory asserts.

aggregated_results.paper_title: Keep CALM and Explore: Language Models for Action Generation in Text-based Games

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: Empirically the paper synthesizes evidence across 28 Jericho games showing that pretrained LM action priors trained on diverse human transcripts generalize to unseen games and, when combined with RL, improve average normalized performance versus several baselines; it quantifies precision/recall behavior across k and reports per-game variability.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The Hybrid Memory Synergy Law claims a meta-regularity: across text-game environments with partial observability and combinatorial action spaces, principled integration of structured and episodic memories produces robust, sample-efficient, and generalizable improvements over single-memory or naive full-history approaches.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: While the paper provides an empirical regularity about LM priors aiding RL across many games, it does not synthesize evidence about the joint contribution of structured and episodic memories; the specific meta-regularity about their combined, systemic benefit is not established here and would require additional cross-method experiments.

aggregated_results.paper_title: RAP: Retrieval-Augmented Planning with Contextual Memory for Multimodal LLM Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper synthesizes empirical evidence across four benchmarks and multiple models, reporting consistent gains from episodic retrieval with adaptive retrieval keys, windowing, and modality-specific embeddings, and presents ablations that identify components contributing to gains.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory asserts a cross-domain meta-regularity: purposeful hybridization of structured and episodic memory will consistently yield robust gains in normalized performance, error reduction, sample efficiency, and generalization relative to single-type or unstructured-history baselines.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: While the paper establishes a cross-benchmark regularity for episodic retrieval, it does not supply empirical synthesis demonstrating the incremental, consistent benefit of adding an explicit structured/relational memory to episodic logs; the hybrid meta-regularity is therefore not supported by the paper's experiments.

aggregated_results.paper_title: Large Language Models Are Neurosymbolic Reasoners

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper provides an empirical dataset showing that LLM agents augmented with symbolic modules and constrained prompts achieve strong, consistent performance across four symbolic tasks, with quantitative comparisons to DRRN and behavior-cloned baselines within the same benchmark suite.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory asserts a meta-regularity: across agents, tasks, and environments, augmenting LLM agents with a hybrid of structured and episodic memory will consistently yield improved robustness, sample efficiency, and generalization relative to single-memory or unstructured-history approaches.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: While the paper synthesizes evidence that tool-augmented LLMs perform well on several symbolic tasks, it does not synthesize cross-study evidence about hybrid memory systems nor present experiments comparing hybrid-memory agents to alternatives; thus the proposed cross-cutting meta-regularity for hybrid memories is not supported by this paper and is a novel synthesis claim.

aggregated_results.paper_title: Graph Constrained Reinforcement Learning for Natural Language Action Spaces

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper establishes an empirical regularity that structured knowledge-graph state representations combined with template action spaces and graph masking tend to improve learning speed and final scores across many IF games, and ablations quantify component contributions.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory posits a broader empirical synthesis: that systematically combining structured and episodic memory stores yields consistent gains in robustness, sample efficiency, reduced error rates, and out-of-distribution generalization over single-memory or unstructured-history baselines.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper supports the value of structured memory across many tasks but provides no empirical data on episodic memory or on hybrid-structured+episodic systems; thus the meta-regularity claimed for hybrid-memory systems is not demonstrated here and is a novel synthesis relative to this paper.

aggregated_results.paper_title: Deciphering Digital Detectives: Understanding LLM Behaviors and Capabilities in Multi-Agent Mystery Games

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper supplies single-domain empirical evidence that MR+SR+SV improves information acquisition and reasoning in Jubensha, and it references related work, but it does not aggregate multi-study results to claim a cross-domain meta-regularity.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory asserts a meta-regularity: across text-game environments, hybrid structured+episodic memory systematically yields superior robustness, sample efficiency, and generalization compared to single or unstructured memories.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: There is no multi-study synthesis or cross-environment aggregation in the paper to support this meta-regularity; claiming such a broad regularity goes beyond the paper's empirical scope and is therefore a genuinely new claim.

aggregated_results.paper_title: Knowledge-enhanced Agents for Interactive Text Games

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper aggregates results across 40 model/task configurations and reports consistent empirical patterns: affordances improve action selection in a majority of cases (25/40), LMs tend to benefit more on average than RL agents, and injecting affordances into KGs is often superior to raw concatenation or separate encoders for KG-A2C.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: It synthesizes these cross-model, cross-task findings into concrete recommendations (prefer affordances over MCA when limited, integrate affordances as KG triples, expect task-dependent variability), producing a meta-regularity about when and how these knowledge types help interactive agents.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: This aggregated empirical regularity across agent classes and tasks—showing affordance-dominant benefits and injection-modality effects on ScienceWorld benchmarks—is a new synthesis specific to this experimental suite and was not previously established in the literature in this consolidated form.

aggregated_results.paper_title: Positive Experience Reflection for Agents in Interactive Text Environments

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper aggregates results across 30 ScienceWorld tasks and three LLM families and reports a consistent empirical pattern: Sweet&Sour improves average success scores, shows larger relative gains on medium-difficulty tasks, and narrows model-size gaps compared to failure-only reflection.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory asserts a broader meta-regularity: hybrid structured+episodic memory yields robust, sample-efficient, and generalizable gains across text-game domains relative to single memory types or unstructured full-history retrieval.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper provides a clear empirical synthesis for episodic/positive-reflection benefits within ScienceWorld, but it does not provide empirical support for the broader hybrid structured+episodic regularity; the hybrid empirical claim is not established here.

aggregated_results.paper_title: A Mixture-of-Experts Approach to Few-Shot Task Transfer in Open-Ended Text Worlds

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: Within its experiments, the paper establishes an empirical regularity: an attention-weighted MoE of frozen KG-A2C experts plus a hot expert consistently speeds few-shot adaptation and improves zero-shot performance in its role-based LIGHT tasks; prior cited work shows KG memory and story-shaping can produce role-aligned behaviors but does not evaluate hybrid structured+episodic combinations.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory claims a broader meta-regularity across agent families and studies: systematic gains from jointly using structured and episodic memories versus single-memory or naive full-history retrieval approaches in text-game agents.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: No cross-study synthesis or experiments involving episodic memory are presented in the paper, so the claimed meta-regularity (structured+episodic hybrid superiority as a general empirical law) is not supported here and is a genuinely new, untested claim relative to this paper.

aggregated_results.paper_title: Reasoning, Memorization, and Fine-Tuning Language Models for Non-Cooperative Games

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: Empirically, the paper establishes that structured summarization plus constrained area selection in a multi-agent tree-of-thought pipeline improves win rates and reduces errors on the tested game, and that task-specific fine-tuning with ≈1k examples yields further gains; it also reports the cost/performance trade-off.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory asserts a broader meta-regularity: across environments and tasks, agents that combine structured relational memory with episodic trajectory memory and retrieve both deliberately will systematically outperform agents with only one memory type or naive full-history retrieval.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper provides no multi-environment synthesis or controlled comparisons among structured-only, episodic-only, unstructured-only, and hybrid-memory agents, so the claimed meta-regularity is not supported by this paper and represents a genuinely new empirical hypothesis relative to the presented results.

aggregated_results.paper_title: diff History for Neural Language Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: Within its experiments, the paper synthesizes evidence showing a consistent empirical pattern: diff-encoded histories provide denser learning signals, improve sample efficiency and generalization, and scale with data in NetHack, with large ablation effects versus full-text history.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory makes a broader meta-regularity claim that hybrid structured+episodic memory systems will systematically outperform single-memory or unstructured-history agents across text-game domains by overcoming partial observability and long-horizon credit assignment bottlenecks.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper provides a strong single-method empirical synthesis for one type of structured episodic encoding (diffs) but does not synthesize cross-method or cross-paper evidence comparing hybrid memory systems to alternatives; the general meta-regularity that hybrid memories will robustly outperform other designs across domains remains an untested, novel empirical claim.

aggregated_results.paper_title: Pre-trained Language Models as Prior Knowledge for Playing Text-based Games

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper provides an empirical synthesis across 14 Jericho games showing a single fine-tuned LM component consistently improves or matches prior baselines on many games, yields better average normalized score, and reduces required environment interactions compared to several baselines.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory asserts a broader meta-regularity: across text-game environments, agents that integrate both structured and episodic memory into working memory will systematically outperform agents that use only one memory type or only unstructured full-history retrieval.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper's synthesis supports the regularity that LM priors help across many games, but it does not synthesize evidence for hybrid structured+episodic memory outperforming other architectures; the hybrid-memory meta-regularity is not demonstrated in the paper and therefore is a novel empirical claim.

unification.what_is_known: The literature extensively documents structured memory systems (knowledge graphs, belief graphs, relational state representations) and episodic memory systems (trajectory logs, reflections, experience replay) for text-game agents, with numerous papers demonstrating benefits of each modality independently or combining them within specific architectures. One recent paper (AriGraph) explicitly formalizes a unified world-model representation G = (V_s, E_s, V_e, E_e) that integrates both semantic and episodic memories with joint retrieval, validating this as a core contribution. However, the majority of papers (10/21) do not engage with this unification principle at all, and eight additional papers implement components from which it could be derived but never explicitly articulate it as a general organizing law.
unification.what_introduced: The theory explicitly asserts a unifying principle that structured semantic/relational memory and episodic trajectory/reflective memory are complementary components of a single hybrid memory architecture whose purposeful integration via joint retrieval systematically produces superior robustness, sample efficiency, and generalization compared to either memory type alone or unstructured alternatives. This frames diverse prior approaches—KG-based methods, episodic reflection systems, and their ad-hoc combinations—as instances of one underlying memory-synergy law applicable across text-game domains with partial observability and long-horizon tasks.
unification.what_novel: The unification principle has been explicitly formalized and validated in one recent paper (AriGraph), making it no longer genuinely novel but not yet widely established or adopted across the field. Two papers treat related unification ideas peripherally (survey cataloging, cross-architecture comparison without theoretical formalization), eight papers contain implementations or empirical patterns from which the principle is derivable through cross-paper synthesis, and ten papers do not address it at all. The elevation of this specific hybrid structured+episodic memory integration to a domain-wide organizing law with systematic performance claims represents knowledge that is explicit in a small portion of the literature but peripheral or unstated in most work.

theory_law_dict.theory_name: Hybrid Structured and Episodic Memory Enables Robust, Generalizable LLM Agent Performance in Text Games
theory_law_dict.theory_description: Language model agents for text-based games achieve superior robustness, sample efficiency, and generalization when augmented with hybrid memory systems that combine explicit structured (e.g., knowledge-graph or belief-graph) state representations and episodic (trajectory or reflection-based) memory. Structured memory provides compact, relational representations that support multi-hop reasoning, navigation, and action-space pruning, while episodic memory enables recall of detailed instructions, procedures, and successful/failed experiences. The synergistic integration and principled retrieval of both kinds of memories enables agents to overcome both partial observability and long-horizon credit assignment bottlenecks, outperforming agents with either memory alone, with unstructured memory (e.g., full text history), or with naive retrieval/summarization.
theory_law_dict.theory_statement: When both structured (e.g., knowledge-graph/semantic) and episodic (e.g., observation/trajectory/reflective) memory are available and purposefully integrated and retrieved into agent working memory, LLM-based agents achieve higher normalized/game performance, reduced error rates, and greater sample efficiency and generalization compared to agents using only one memory type or only unstructured full-history/retrieval.
theory_law_dict.domain_scope: Applies to LLM agents in text-based games with partial observability, combinatorial action spaces, and complex state transitions, especially in environments requiring multi-step planning, navigation, and long-term instruction following (e.g., TextWorld, NetHack, general text-based tasks)
special_cases: Games solvable via short-term context or trivial observation-to-action mapping may show little benefit.
If memory construction (e.g., triplet extraction) is highly unreliable, benefits are reduced/disrupted.

aggregated_results.paper_title: AriGraph: Learning Knowledge Graph World Models with Episodic Memory for LLM Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper explicitly unifies semantic (knowledge-graph) and episodic memories into a single world-model formalism G = (V_s, E_s, V_e, E_e) and motivates this with cognitive-memory analogies and algorithmic integration (joint retrieval feeding a single working memory).
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory frames multiple prior memory approaches (structured KG, episodic logs, unstructured RAG/full-history) as instances that can be subsumed by a hybrid integrated-memory principle explaining superior agent behavior.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: This unification is central to the paper: the authors present the combined graph abstraction, retrieval pipeline, and experiments showing that the integration (not either memory alone) drives performance; thus the unification claim is explicit and established in this work.

aggregated_results.paper_title: A Survey on Large Language Model-Based Game Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper explicitly unifies a broad literature under a reference architecture (memory, reasoning, perception–action), categorizes numerous agents by memory design (Table 3), and highlights common mechanisms (consolidation, structuring, retrieval) across environments and tasks.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory proposes a unifying principle that many disparate agent designs and observed benefits can be seen as instances of a single 'hybrid memory synergy' framework where joint structured+episodic memory yields superior robustness and generalization.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: While the survey provides a clear high-level unification and catalogs memory modalities and uses, it does not elevate the particular hybrid structured+episodic memory claim as the central unifying thesis nor present it as an explicit organizing law; treating hybrid memory as the primary unifier is therefore a conceptual emphasis that goes beyond the paper's explicit framing.

aggregated_results.paper_title: Knowledge-enhanced Agents for Interactive Text Games

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: Prior work has applied KGs, affordances, and action-history in various isolated ways to text-game or embodied agents (KG-A2C, DRRN variants, LM history inputs, etc.), but these were typically studied within single architectures or single injection modalities.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The paper provides a single experimental framework that systematically injects the same two knowledge types (affordances and MCA) into multiple training paradigms (RL and LM), multiple architectures, and through multiple injection modalities, enabling direct cross-paradigm comparison.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: This cross-architecture, cross-injection comparative evaluation pragmatically unifies disparate prior approaches at the empirical level (showing common patterns and differences), but it stops short of a formal theoretical unification.

aggregated_results.paper_title: MemInsight: Autonomous Memory Augmentation for LLM Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper situates MemInsight relative to prior methods (RAG, MemoryBank, DPR, other memory pipelines) and shows attribute-based augmentations can complement embedding/RAG-style retrieval, but it treats these as distinct techniques rather than formally unifying them.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory explicitly proposes a unifying principle that structured (knowledge-graph/semantic) and episodic (trajectory/experience) memories are components of a single hybrid memory architecture whose integration explains superior robustness, sample-efficiency, and generalization.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: While the paper hints that structured augmentations complement unstructured history and RAG, it does not formalize or validate a domain-wide unification of diverse memory paradigms into a single hybrid principle; the unification claim is therefore not established by the paper.

aggregated_results.paper_title: A Mixture-of-Experts Approach to Few-Shot Task Transfer in Open-Ended Text Worlds

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper explicitly unifies two technical elements in a single architecture: knowledge-graph (structured) persistent state representations (KG-A2C family) and an MoE policy composition mechanism (frozen experts + trainable expert + attention), demonstrating that these can be combined for few-shot role transfer in text worlds.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory-level claim is a broader unification that structured relational memory and episodic trajectory memory are complementary instances of a single hybrid-memory principle whose joint retrieval systematically improves robustness and generalization for LLM-based agents in text games.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: That higher-level conceptual unification (structured + episodic memory as a single operative principle) is not made or empirically supported in the paper — the paper only unifies KG-based structured memory with MoE policy reuse, not an episodic memory component — so the theory's unification is not present in the paper.

aggregated_results.paper_title: Generative Agents: Interactive Simulacra of Human Behavior

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper explicitly unifies observation logging, retrieval, reflection (abstraction), and hierarchical planning into a single agent architecture and empirically shows the joint system outperforms ablations that remove one or more components, arguing that their combination produces long-term coherence and emergent social dynamics.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The Hybrid Memory Synergy Law explicitly unifies two distinct memory modalities—structured semantic/state representations and episodic reflective trajectories—into a single integrated retrieval-and-working-memory framework whose combined use is claimed to produce synergistic gains over either modality alone or over naïve unstructured full-history retrieval.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper contains an environment tree used for grounding and an episodic natural-language memory pipeline, but it does not present, formalize, or experimentally validate the specific conceptual unification of a semantic/graph-structured memory tightly integrated with an episodic reflective memory as a single retrievable substrate; that precise unification claim is not made or tested here.

aggregated_results.paper_title: Monte Carlo Planning with Large Language Model for Text-Based Game Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper situates MC-DML relative to prior threads (KG/state-structure methods, MCTS+RL hybrids, LLM-as-prior methods) and unifies short-term trajectory context and cross-trial reflection as two complementary memory types used inside an LLM prior for MCTS.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: It explicitly unifies in-trial (working) memory and cross-trial (episodic/reflection) memory within an MCTS+LLM framework, showing their combined benefit empirically.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The theory's stronger unification claim — that structured relational memory (e.g., knowledge graphs) and episodic/reflective memory are instances of a single hybrid memory whose principled joint retrieval yields the reported benefits — is not realized or tested in the paper; achieving that full unification would require integrating KG-style representations with the reflection mechanism.

aggregated_results.paper_title: Keep CALM and Explore: Language Models for Action Generation in Text-based Games

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper situates CALM relative to two distinct prior strands: (1) linguistic-prior/action-affordance approaches (n-grams, Word2Vec, seq2seq) and (2) structured-graph/knowledge-graph RL approaches (KG-A2C), and it empirically compares CALM to both kinds of baselines. It thus highlights complementary strengths: LMs provide linguistic priors while KGs provide structured state augmentation.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The Hybrid Memory Synergy Law explicitly asserts a unification: that structured knowledge-graph style state representations and episodic trajectory/reflective memories are instances of complementary components of a single hybrid memory architecture whose integration produces superior agent performance.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: Although the paper compares and contrasts LM-based and graph-based methods (so the idea of complementarity is implicitly present), it does not explicitly formulate or prove a unifying principle that subsumes both as parts of one hybrid memory framework nor empirically validate that their integration yields the claimed unified improvements.

aggregated_results.paper_title: RAP: Retrieval-Augmented Planning with Contextual Memory for Multimodal LLM Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper explicitly unifies retrieval-augmented episodic memory, planning, and multimodal perception into an operational pipeline (Memory, Reasoner, Retriever, Executor) and situates RAP relative to RAG, ReAct, Reflexion, and ExpeL, showing that retrieval+planning can work across textual and embodied tasks.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory asserts a higher-level unification: that structured relational memory and episodic instance memory are instances of a single hybrid memory abstraction whose joint, principled retrieval is the core principle driving robustness and generalization in LLM agents.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: While the paper's logs contain structured fields (task, plan) combined with episodic instances, it does not formalize or experimentally validate the proposed canonical unification of an explicit KG-style structured memory with episodic trajectories as a single memory abstraction.

aggregated_results.paper_title: Pre-trained Language Models as Prior Knowledge for Playing Text-based Games

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper situates itself relative to prior approaches that use explicit knowledge graphs, object-centric retrieval, and action-generation LMs and empirically shows that LM-derived representations can match or outperform several KG-based and other baselines on many games, suggesting representational priors from LMs can substitute for some KG functionality.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory explicitly asserts a unifying principle: structured (KG-like) approaches and episodic (trajectory/reflective) retrieval approaches are complementary and should be combined into a single hybrid memory framework whose integration explains superior agent performance across text-game tasks.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: While the paper compares LM-based and KG-based methods and cites both lines of work, it does not explicitly state or formalize a unification that both memory types are complementary and that their integration yields the combined benefit; such an explicit unification can be plausibly synthesized from literature but is not made or tested in the paper.

aggregated_results.paper_title: Learning Dynamic Belief Graphs to Generalize on Text-Based Games

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper integrates several existing components—dynamic graph extraction from text, continuous multi-relational belief representations, R-GCN encoding, transformer text encoders, attention-based aggregation, and reinforcement learning—into a single end-to-end agent (GATA) that learns to infer and use latent belief graphs for decision making.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory-level claim proposes a unification that structured (KG-like) memory and episodic (trajectory/experience) memory are complementary instances of a single hybrid-memory principle whose deliberate integration and retrieval drives robustness and generalization in LLM agents.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: While the paper concretely unifies graph-structured state estimation with policy learning, it does not explicitly articulate or demonstrate the broader unification that pairs structured memory with an independent episodic memory as a single necessary principle; that higher-level unification is not shown in the paper.

aggregated_results.paper_title: Large Language Models Are Neurosymbolic Reasoners

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper unifies several prior ideas—LLM prompting, constrained prompt formats, and adding symbolic modules into the action space—into an operational neurosymbolic agent for text-based games, showing this combination is effective on the benchmark tasks.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The Hybrid Memory Synergy Law claims an explicit unification: structured relational representations and episodic (trajectory/reflective) traces should be treated as components of a single, synergistic hybrid-memory principle that explains improved robustness, sample efficiency, and generalization of LLM agents.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper does not propose or formalize such a unifying hybrid-memory principle that subsumes structured-state representations and episodic traces; it neither implements combined structured+episodic memory nor frames prior results as instances of one underlying memory-principle. The unification claim is therefore absent from this work.

aggregated_results.paper_title: Graph Constrained Reinforcement Learning for Natural Language Action Spaces

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper explicitly unifies a knowledge-graph-based state representation with a template-based, graph-masked action generation pipeline and argues that their combination enables scalable exploration of huge natural-language action spaces.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory asserts a broader unifying principle that multiple prior approaches (structured relational state representations and episodic/unstructured trajectory memory) should be treated as complementary components of a single hybrid memory architecture whose integration yields robust, generalizable LLM agents in text-game domains.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper already unifies state structure and action templates, but it neither includes episodic memory components nor claims that structured and episodic memories are instances of one unified memory framework; this higher-level unification is not present in the paper.

aggregated_results.paper_title: Deciphering Digital Detectives: Understanding LLM Behaviors and Capabilities in Multi-Agent Mystery Games

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper situates its design relative to prior retrieval-augmented, self-consistency, and in-context learning work and treats MR, SR, SV as complementary components within a single architecture for Jubensha.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory-level claim is a unification: structured relational memory and episodic trajectory memory are instances of a single synergistic memory framework that explains and improves agent behavior across tasks.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper does not propose or demonstrate this conceptual unification between explicit structured (relational) memory and episodic memory; it evaluates only unstructured episodic retrieval plus refinement/verification, so the proposed unified principle is absent from the paper.

aggregated_results.paper_title: Positive Experience Reflection for Agents in Interactive Text Environments

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The literature contains separate lines on graph/structured approaches for text games and on episodic/self-reflective LLM agents; the paper cites both but implements and evaluates only the episodic/reflection/managed-memory approach (Sweet&Sour).
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory explicitly unifies structured (knowledge-graph-like) and episodic (trajectory/reflection) memories into a single principle asserting synergistic gains when both are purposefully integrated and retrieved.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper does not perform or evaluate this unification, nor present empirical or theoretical evidence that combining structured relational state representations with episodic reflections outperforms either alone; that unification claim is not addressed and is novel relative to this paper.

aggregated_results.paper_title: Behavior Cloned Transformers are Neurosymbolic Reasoners

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper unifies a practical technique—action-space injection of symbolic-module actions—with behavior cloning across multiple module types and tasks, showing a single operational method works for arithmetic, navigation, sorting, and KB queries in text games.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory asserts a higher-level unification: that structured relational memory and episodic trajectory/reflective memory are instances of a single, synergistic memory-augmentation framework whose joint integration explains improved agent robustness and generalization.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper does not formalize or argue this kind of conceptual unification between structured and episodic memory systems; thus the unification claim in the theory is not present in or supported by the paper.

aggregated_results.paper_title: Reasoning, Memorization, and Fine-Tuning Language Models for Non-Cooperative Games

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper explicitly unifies two operational ideas: tree-of-thought search and a multi-agent task-decomposition pipeline, and it couples online agent interactions with an offline fine-tuning loop to improve performance. These elements are presented and experimentally evaluated together in a single pipeline.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory explicitly unifies two memory paradigms—structured relational state representations and episodic trajectory/experience memory—into a single claim that their integrated retrieval is the key principle giving robust, generalizable agent behavior.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper does not perform a unifying synthesis of structured versus episodic memory paradigms nor run controlled comparisons that treat those two memory types as components of a single framework; the specific unification of structured + episodic memory is therefore not present and represents a genuine novelty relative to this paper.

aggregated_results.paper_title: Reflexion: Language Agents with Verbal Reinforcement Learning

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper unifies several prior ideas into a single framework by formalizing an Actor/Evaluator/Self-Reflection loop and distinguishing short-term trajectory history from long-term textual reflections as memory components.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory explicitly asserts a unifying principle that structured (semantic/graph) memories and episodic (trajectory/reflective) memories are instances of a single hybrid memory architecture whose combined operation explains improved robustness and generalization.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: Reflexion does not claim or empirically support that structured and episodic memories are unified under one overarching principle nor that their combination subsumes prior methods; this explicit unification is not present in the paper and thus is a novel claim relative to Reflexion.

aggregated_results.paper_title: Learning Knowledge Graph-based World Models of Textual Environments

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper explicitly unifies two tasks—knowledge-graph state prediction and valid-action generation—into a single multi-task transformer architecture and reframes both outputs as Set-of-Sequences, showing they are mutually informative for world modeling.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory asserts a unification at the level of memory modalities: that structured and episodic memories should be treated as complementary components of a single hybrid-memory principle for LLM agents, with joint retrieval and integration into working memory producing systematic benefits.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper does not claim or empirically evaluate unifying structured and episodic memory modalities; its unification is between prediction tasks, not memory systems. The proposed memory-modality unification is therefore a new conceptual unification relative to this work.

aggregated_results.paper_title: Language Models are Few-Shot Butlers

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper explicitly unifies imitation learning and reinforcement-style self-improvement by optimizing the same action-modeling objective on both expert demonstrations and successful agent-generated trajectories (iterated action modeling), connecting two training paradigms within one framework.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory asserts a different unification: that structured-memory approaches (e.g., knowledge graphs/semantic state) and episodic-memory approaches (trajectory/reflective memory) can be seen as instances of a single underlying architectural principle whose purposeful integration yields synergistic agent benefits.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper does not perform a conceptual or empirical unification of structured and episodic memory modalities; its unification is orthogonal (imitation + self-imitation). Therefore the proposed unification of memory modalities is not present in the paper and is novel relative to it.

aggregated_results.paper_title: Thespian: Multi-Character Text Role-Playing Game Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: This paper unifies several technical ideas for multi-behavior text-RL: soft prompt conditioning, multi-headed actor/critic outputs (one head per character), and an attention-based fusion module for few-shot composition, built on a KG-A2C structured-memory base.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory states an explicit unification principle that structured (relational) and episodic (trajectory/reflection) memories are complementary components of a single hybrid-memory framework whose joint use explains and improves agent behavior in text games.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper does not claim or demonstrate that structured and episodic memories are instances of one unified hybrid principle nor show cross-cutting instances from prior work that this hybrid principle subsumes, so the unification claim is not present in the paper.

aggregated_results.paper_title: diff History for Neural Language Agents

dimension_evaluation.what_is_known: The paper situates diff history among prior abstraction techniques (e.g., Scratchpad, Chain-of-Thought) and shows that a task-agnostic, procedural textual abstraction can improve LM agent learning and generalization, implicitly relating representation compression and relational abstraction across environments.
dimension_evaluation.what_introduced: The theory asserts an explicit unification claim: that structured (knowledge/semantic graphs) and episodic (trajectory) memories are complementary modalities whose principled combination is a single underlying mechanism driving robust, generalizable LLM-agent performance.
dimension_evaluation.what_novel: The paper does not assert or demonstrate this explicit unification across diverse memory formalisms; it evaluates one specific structured-episodic encoding (diff deltas) but does not show that graph-style structured memories and episodic traces are instances of one unifying principle or that they jointly implement the same mechanism. The unification claim is therefore not present in the paper.

phenomenon_effect.what_is_known: A subset of papers (AriGraph, Knowledge-enhanced Agents, Learning Dynamic Belief Graphs, Positive Experience Reflection, MoE, and others) have explicitly demonstrated through controlled experiments that augmenting LLM agents with both structured memory (knowledge graphs, belief graphs, affordances) and episodic memory (trajectory logs, reflections, action histories) yields measurably higher performance metrics (normalized scores, success rates, sample efficiency) compared to single-memory or unstructured baselines across multiple text-game benchmarks (TextWorld, ScienceWorld, NetHack, Jericho, LIGHT). These demonstrations include quantitative comparisons and ablations isolating memory-component contributions.
phenomenon_effect.what_introduced: The theory statement formalizes and names a specific empirical regularity as the 'Hybrid Memory Synergy Law': that purposeful integration and retrieval of both structured (knowledge-graph/semantic) and episodic (observation/trajectory/reflective) memory into agent working memory systematically produces superior performance, reduced error rates, and enhanced sample efficiency and generalization in partially observable text games with combinatorial action spaces and long-horizon requirements, with specified boundary conditions (minimal benefit in short-context games; disruption when memory construction is unreliable).
phenomenon_effect.what_novel: While 6 papers (~27%) explicitly demonstrate this hybrid-memory performance benefit with experimental evidence, 11 papers (50%) do not test or measure this architecture at all, and comprehensive surveys classify the general principle as derivable rather than established. The phenomenon has been repeatedly demonstrated in specific implementations but has not been widely recognized, named, or treated as established knowledge across the field. The theory's contribution is elevating scattered empirical findings into an explicit, named law with formal scope and boundary conditions, rather than introducing a genuinely new empirical observation.

explanatory_mechanistic.what_is_known: One paper (AriGraph, 2024) provides a complete, explicit implementation and validation of the dual-memory mechanistic account: structured KG memory (V_s, E_s for semantic triplets) combined with episodic memory (V_e, E_e for observation traces), integrated via concrete two-stage retrieval algorithms, demonstrating that their principled integration causally produces superior performance in text-based games. One earlier paper (Knowledge-enhanced Agents) describes mechanistic roles for structured KGs and episodic traces but treats these as applications of existing known mechanisms (affordances, MCA) rather than highlighting their synergy as a novel discovery. A survey paper and several theoretical works describe the separate functional contributions of each memory type and their consolidation/retrieval mechanisms, making the integrated causal claim derivable but not explicitly stated as a validated principle.
explanatory_mechanistic.what_introduced: The theory articulates a specific causal decomposition asserting that structured memory enables compact relational representations supporting multi-hop reasoning, navigation, and action-space pruning, while episodic memory preserves detailed procedural/contextual traces aiding long-horizon credit assignment and instruction following; crucially, it claims that principled joint retrieval of both into working memory synergistically resolves partial observability and long-horizon bottlenecks. This complete mechanistic account—specifying distinct causal roles, their complementary functions, and the retrieval-mediated synergy—is explicitly instantiated and empirically validated in AriGraph (2024), anticipated peripherally in one prior paper, and derivable from survey/theoretical syntheses, but was not proposed or tested in the majority of work.
explanatory_mechanistic.what_novel: The complete, validated mechanistic account is explicit and established as of AriGraph (2024), which provides full empirical support for the theory's causal claims. However, relative to the broader field, this mechanistic account was genuinely novel: 68% of papers (15/22) do not propose, implement, or validate this dual-memory causal mechanism, instead offering alternative explanations (LM priors, tool use, compression, reflection-only, graph-only mechanisms, or multi-agent decomposition). An additional 23% (5/22) found the mechanism derivable but unstated, meaning it could be inferred from their components but was not explicitly formulated or tested. Only one paper beyond AriGraph treated elements of this mechanism as known (explicit_peripheral). The mechanistic novelty is therefore temporally concentrated: it represents an explicit, validated contribution as of 2024, but was largely absent or only implicit in prior literature.

unification.what_is_known: The literature extensively documents structured memory systems (knowledge graphs, belief graphs, relational state representations) and episodic memory systems (trajectory logs, reflections, experience replay) for text-game agents, with numerous papers demonstrating benefits of each modality independently or combining them within specific architectures. One recent paper (AriGraph) explicitly formalizes a unified world-model representation G = (V_s, E_s, V_e, E_e) that integrates both semantic and episodic memories with joint retrieval, validating this as a core contribution. However, the majority of papers (10/21) do not engage with this unification principle at all, and eight additional papers implement components from which it could be derived but never explicitly articulate it as a general organizing law.
unification.what_introduced: The theory explicitly asserts a unifying principle that structured semantic/relational memory and episodic trajectory/reflective memory are complementary components of a single hybrid memory architecture whose purposeful integration via joint retrieval systematically produces superior robustness, sample efficiency, and generalization compared to either memory type alone or unstructured alternatives. This frames diverse prior approaches—KG-based methods, episodic reflection systems, and their ad-hoc combinations—as instances of one underlying memory-synergy law applicable across text-game domains with partial observability and long-horizon tasks.
unification.what_novel: The unification principle has been explicitly formalized and validated in one recent paper (AriGraph), making it no longer genuinely novel but not yet widely established or adopted across the field. Two papers treat related unification ideas peripherally (survey cataloging, cross-architecture comparison without theoretical formalization), eight papers contain implementations or empirical patterns from which the principle is derivable through cross-paper synthesis, and ten papers do not address it at all. The elevation of this specific hybrid structured+episodic memory integration to a domain-wide organizing law with systematic performance claims represents knowledge that is explicit in a small portion of the literature but peripheral or unstated in most work.

generalization_scope_expansion.what_is_known: The AriGraph paper explicitly demonstrates and validates hybrid structured (knowledge-graph) + episodic memory integration across multiple interactive text-game families (Treasure Hunt, Cleaning, Cooking, constrained NetHack) and static multi-hop QA benchmarks (MuSiQue, HotpotQA), reporting robust cross-domain performance, scaling behavior, and treating this cross-domain generalization as a primary contribution. One additional paper (Knowledge-enhanced Agents) provides explicit but peripheral multi-task evaluation across ScienceWorld with both RL and LM paradigms.
generalization_scope_expansion.what_introduced: The theory statement claims that hybrid structured+episodic memory integration enables superior normalized performance, reduced error rates, and greater sample efficiency and generalization for LLM agents specifically across text-based games with partial observability, combinatorial action spaces, and complex state transitions (e.g., TextWorld, NetHack, general text-based tasks requiring multi-step planning, navigation, and long-horizon instruction following), compared to single-memory or unstructured approaches.
generalization_scope_expansion.what_novel: The scope expansion is not novel: AriGraph has already explicitly demonstrated, evaluated, and reported the claimed cross-domain applicability of hybrid structured+episodic memory for LLM agents across representative target environments (interactive text games including constrained NetHack, plus reasoning benchmarks), establishing this generalization as known work. While 21 other papers either suggest the scope would be derivable or genuinely new relative to their more limited experimental domains, this does not override the fact that one paper has already explicitly established and validated the core scope claim as a recognized contribution.

constraint_limitation.what_is_known: Multiple foundational papers (AriGraph, Reflexion, Learning Dynamic Belief Graphs, comprehensive surveys, and MemInsight) explicitly document and empirically investigate the constraints listed in the theory through ablations and failure-mode analyses: minimal benefit for trivial short-horizon tasks solvable from short-term context, and degraded performance when structured-memory construction (triplet extraction, graph building) is unreliable or noisy. These constraints are treated as recognized practical limitations with quantified effects across task types, model capacities, and extraction-quality regimes.
constraint_limitation.what_introduced: The theory enumerates two explicit special-case constraints as boundary conditions for the Hybrid Memory Synergy Law: (1) games solvable via short-term context or trivial observation-to-action mapping may show little benefit from hybrid memory, and (2) if memory construction (e.g., triplet extraction) is highly unreliable, benefits are reduced or disrupted.
constraint_limitation.what_novel: The constraints are not novel relative to the established literature. Five leading papers explicitly establish these failure modes as known limitations with empirical evidence; eleven additional papers acknowledge overlapping constraints peripherally. While five papers rate these constraints as derivable-unstated or genuinely-new (indicating they didn't analyze them), the preponderance of evidence—especially from foundational works including a comprehensive survey—demonstrates that these constraints are already explicit, empirically validated, and recognized as practical limitations in the field.

conceptual_reframing_abstraction.what_is_known: The field exhibits substantial prior work on memory components for LLM text-game agents: one paper (AriGraph) explicitly formalizes a hybrid graph+episodic memory architecture with integrated retrieval pipelines as its core contribution; comprehensive surveys catalog memory abstractions (working/long-term, chunks/KV/trees/graphs/parametric) and operations (consolidation, structuring, retrieval); taxonomies organize knowledge types (structured affordance vs episodic action-memory) and injection strategies; and multiple papers implement either structured state representations (knowledge graphs, belief graphs) or episodic memory components (reflections, trajectory buffers) as individual design choices without formalizing their complementary integration.
conceptual_reframing_abstraction.what_introduced: The theory elevates the purposeful integration of hybrid structured (relational/semantic) and episodic (trajectory/reflective) memory with principled retrieval into working memory as the canonical, general organizing abstraction for robust LLM agent design in text games. It reframes this not as a collection of independent techniques or a specialized solution but as a fundamental architectural law prescribing synergistic combination of relational semantic state and trajectory/reflective experience as the core design principle for overcoming partial observability and long-horizon credit assignment across diverse text-game environments.
conceptual_reframing_abstraction.what_novel: The conceptual reframing exhibits a clear gradient: it is the explicit core organizing framework in one specialized paper (AriGraph, 1/23); it appears as peripheral/cataloged content in surveys and taxonomies that enumerate but don't center this integration (3/23); it is derivable but unstated from papers implementing structured or episodic components separately without formalizing their complementary synergistic roles (6/23); and it is genuinely novel—representing an entirely different organizing principle—relative to the majority of the field (13/23) focused on neurosymbolic reasoning, action generation, ToM planning, transfer learning, and other frameworks. Overall, while explicitly instantiated in isolated specialized work, the abstraction remains unstated as a general field-wide design principle and is derivable from but not recognized in the collective body of component-focused research.

empirical_synthesis_meta_regulariry.what_is_known: One paper (AriGraph) explicitly establishes the hybrid structured+episodic memory meta-regularity through comprehensive experiments across TextWorld, NetHack, and QA benchmarks with full ablations confirming synergistic contributions. Ten papers provide partial evidence by demonstrating consistent benefits of either structured memory alone (e.g., belief graphs, KG-constrained RL) or episodic memory alone (e.g., verbal reflections, trajectory retrieval) within their respective experimental suites, showing reproducible within-modality improvements. One paper (GATA) explicitly demonstrates structured memory benefits but discusses the full hybrid pattern only peripherally.
empirical_synthesis_meta_regulariry.what_introduced: The theory asserts a general cross-domain empirical meta-regularity: purposeful integration of both structured (knowledge-graph/semantic) and episodic (trajectory/reflective) memory into LLM agent working memory systematically yields superior normalized performance, reduced errors, and greater sample efficiency/generalization compared to single-memory-type, unstructured-history, or naive-retrieval agents across text-based games with partial observability and complex state transitions. This positions the hybrid synergy as a reproducible architectural design principle rather than a task-specific optimization.
empirical_synthesis_meta_regulariry.what_novel: The meta-regularity is explicitly validated in one comprehensive study (AriGraph) but has not achieved broad recognition or independent replication across the wider text-game agent literature: 10 of 21 papers demonstrate benefits of individual memory types from which the hybrid principle could be derived but do not state or test it, and 9 papers provide no empirical basis for the hybrid claim at all. While rigorously demonstrated in at least one major work, the principle is not yet treated as established or central organizing knowledge by the field, indicating it remains at the periphery of collective awareness despite strong empirical support in one paper.
